title: The Tool-Overuse Illusion: Why Does LLM Prefer External Tools over Internal Knowledge?
summary: Equipping LLMs with external tools effectively addresses internal reasoning limitations. However, it introduces a critical yet under-explored phenomenon: tool overuse, the unnecessary tool-use during reasoning. In this paper, we first reveal this phenomenon is pervasive across diverse LLMs. We then experimentally elucidate its underlying mechanisms through two key lenses: (1) First, by analyzing tool-use behavior across different internal knowledge availability regions, we identify a \textit{knowledge epistemic illusion}: models misjudge internal knowledge boundaries and fail to accurately perceive their actual knowledge availability. To mitigate this, we propose a knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment strategy based on direct preference optimization, which reduces tool usage in by 82.8\% while yielding an accuracy improvement. (2) Second, we establish a causal link between reward structures and tool-use behavior by visualizing the tool-augmented training process. It reveals that \textit{outcome-only rewards} inadvertently encourage tool overuse by rewarding only final correctness, regardless of tool efficiency. To verify this, we balance reward signals during training rather than relying on outcome-only rewards, cutting unnecessary tool calls by 66.7\% (7B) and 60.7\% (32B) without sacrificing accuracy. Finally, we provide theoretical justification in this two lenses to understand tool overuse.

AI.tldr: The paper studies and mitigates “tool overuse” in tool-augmented LLMs, showing it is pervasive, diagnosing why it happens, and proposing training strategies that drastically reduce unnecessary tool calls while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: While external tools help LLMs overcome internal reasoning and knowledge limits, they also cause a new, understudied issue: models call tools even when not needed, wasting latency, cost, and sometimes degrading reasoning. There is little systematic understanding of why this overuse happens or how to correct it without hurting performance.
AI.method: 1) Empirically measure tool-use patterns across various LLMs and knowledge regimes to show pervasiveness of tool overuse. 2) Analyze behavior as a function of internal knowledge availability and identify a “knowledge epistemic illusion” where models misjudge what they already know. 3) Propose a knowledge-aware epistemic boundary alignment strategy based on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that trains models to better distinguish when to rely on internal vs external knowledge. 4) Visualize and analyze the training dynamics under different reward designs in tool-augmented RL/finetuning to show that outcome-only rewards spur overuse. 5) Introduce a balanced reward scheme that explicitly incorporates tool efficiency, not just final correctness, and evaluate reductions in tool calls and accuracy impact. 6) Provide theoretical arguments under both lenses (epistemic boundaries and reward structures) explaining why overuse arises and why the proposed fixes work.
AI.result: They show tool overuse is widespread among tool-augmented LLMs. Their DPO-based epistemic boundary alignment reduces tool usage by 82.8% while *increasing* accuracy. Their balanced reward design lowers unnecessary tool calls by 66.7% for a 7B model and 60.7% for a 32B model, with no accuracy loss. Visual analyses confirm a causal relationship between reward structure and overuse behavior.
AI.conclusion: Tool overuse is a systematic, not incidental, behavior of current tool-augmented LLMs, arising from miscalibrated epistemic boundaries and poorly shaped rewards. By aligning models’ perception of their internal knowledge and rebalancing reward signals to account for tool efficiency, one can drastically cut unnecessary tool invocations without sacrificing—and sometimes improving—task accuracy. The paper offers both empirical and theoretical foundations for designing more cost-efficient and better-calibrated tool-using LLMs.

title: AI to Learn 2.0: A Deliverable-Oriented Governance Framework and Maturity Rubric for Opaque AI in Learning-Intensive Domains
summary: Generative AI is entering research, education, and professional work faster than current governance frameworks can specify how AI-assisted outputs should be judged in learning-intensive settings. The central problem is proxy failure: a polished artifact can be useful while no longer serving as credible evidence of the human understanding, judgment, or transfer ability that the work is supposed to cultivate or certify. This paper proposes AI to Learn 2.0, a deliverable-oriented governance framework for AI-assisted work. Rather than claiming element-wise novelty, it reorganizes adjacent ideas around the final deliverable package, distinguishes artifact residual from capability residual, and operationalizes the result through a five-part package, a seven-dimension maturity rubric, gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and a companion capability-evidence ladder. AI to Learn 2.0 allows opaque AI during exploration, drafting, hypothesis generation, and workflow design, but requires that the released deliverable be usable, auditable, transferable, and justifiable without the original large language model or cloud API. In learning-intensive contexts, it additionally requires context-appropriate human-attributable evidence of explanation or transfer. Worked scoring across contrastive cases, including coursework substitution, a symbolic-regression governance contrast, teacher-audited national-exam practice forms, and a self-hosted lecture-to-quiz pipeline with deterministic quality control, shows how the framework separates polished substitution workflows from bounded, auditable, and handoff-ready AI-assisted workflows. AI to Learn 2.0 is proposed as a governance instrument for structured third-party review where capability preservation, accountability, and validity boundaries matter.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes 'AI to Learn 2.0', a governance framework to judge AI-assisted work, ensuring outputs remain valid evidence of human capability.
AI.motivation: Existing governance frameworks lag behind generative AI adoption, causing 'proxy failure' where polished AI-assisted outputs no longer reliably indicate human understanding, judgment, or ability to transfer knowledge, especially in learning-intensive settings.
AI.method: The authors design a deliverable-oriented governance framework centered on the final deliverable package. They distinguish artifact residual (what remains usable without AI) from capability residual (what humans can still do without AI) and encode this in a five-part deliverable package, a seven-dimension maturity rubric with gate thresholds on critical dimensions, and a capability-evidence ladder. They illustrate its application through worked scoring of contrastive cases (e.g., coursework substitution, exam prep forms, symbolic regression governance, and a self-hosted lecture-to-quiz pipeline).
AI.result: The framework cleanly distinguishes between AI-enabled substitution workflows (where AI replaces human learning/effort) and AI-assisted workflows that remain bounded, auditable, and ready for handoff. The worked examples demonstrate how the rubric and thresholds can be applied to concrete educational and professional scenarios to preserve capability evidence and accountability.
AI.conclusion: AI to Learn 2.0 offers a practical governance instrument for AI-assisted work, especially in learning-intensive contexts, by requiring that final deliverables be usable and auditable without the original AI system and, where appropriate, accompanied by human-attributable evidence of understanding or transfer. It is intended for structured third-party review where maintaining human capability, accountability, and validity of evidence is critical.

title: Algorithm Selection with Zero Domain Knowledge via Text Embeddings
summary: We propose a feature-free approach to algorithm selection that replaces hand-crafted instance features with pretrained text embeddings. Our method, ZeroFolio, proceeds in three steps: it reads the raw instance file as plain text, embeds it with a pretrained embedding model, and selects an algorithm via weighted k-nearest neighbors. The key to our approach is the observation that pretrained embeddings produce representations that distinguish problem instances without any domain knowledge or task-specific training. This allows us to apply the same three-step pipeline (serialize, embed, select) across diverse problem domains with text-based instance formats. We evaluate our approach on 11 ASlib scenarios spanning 7 domains (SAT, MaxSAT, QBF, ASP, CSP, MIP, and graph problems). Our experiments show that this approach outperforms a random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10 of 11 scenarios with a single fixed configuration, and in all 11 with two-seed voting; the margin is often substantial. Our ablation study shows that inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and Manhattan distance are the key design choices. On scenarios where both selectors are competitive, combining embeddings with hand-crafted features via soft voting yields further improvements.

AI.tldr: ZeroFolio replaces hand-crafted instance features for algorithm selection with pretrained text embeddings of raw problem instances and uses a simple k-NN selector, outperforming traditional feature-based random forests on most benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional algorithm selection relies on hand-crafted, domain-specific instance features, which are costly to design, maintain, and compute, and may not transfer well across domains. With the rise of powerful pretrained embedding models, there is an opportunity to avoid manual feature engineering and build a more generic, plug-and-play algorithm selection pipeline that can work across many problem domains that already use textual instance formats.
AI.method: ZeroFolio converts each problem instance into plain text (serialization), encodes this text using a pretrained text embedding model to obtain a vector representation, and then performs algorithm selection using a weighted k-nearest-neighbors (k-NN) scheme in the embedding space. The design emphasizes being feature-free (no domain features), using inverse-distance weighting and Manhattan distance in k-NN, and optionally shuffling lines of the instance to improve robustness. The approach is evaluated on multiple ASlib scenarios and analyzed via ablation studies to understand which components matter most.
AI.result: Across 11 ASlib scenarios from 7 problem domains (SAT, MaxSAT, QBF, ASP, CSP, MIP, and graph problems), ZeroFolio with a single fixed configuration beats a random forest trained on hand-crafted features in 10 out of 11 scenarios, and with a two-seed voting variant it wins in all 11. The improvements are often large. Ablation experiments identify inverse-distance weighting, line shuffling, and using Manhattan distance as the most important design choices. When both the embedding-based and feature-based selectors perform similarly, combining them via soft voting yields further performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained text embeddings of raw instance files are sufficient to drive effective algorithm selection, removing the need for hand-crafted instance features and domain expertise. A simple, generic three-step pipeline (serialize, embed, select) can outperform a strong feature-based baseline across a wide range of domains using text-based formats. Furthermore, careful choices in the k-NN configuration and simple ensemble combinations with traditional feature-based methods can provide additional benefits, suggesting that text embeddings are a powerful, domain-agnostic basis for algorithm selection.

title: Evidence of Layered Positional and Directional Constraints in the Voynich Manuscript: Implications for Cipher-Like Structure
summary:
The Voynich Manuscript (VMS) exhibits a script of uncertain origin whose grapheme sequences have resisted linguistic analysis. We present a systematic analysis of its grapheme sequences, revealing two complementary structural layers: a character-level right-to-left optimization in word-internal sequences and a left-to-right dependency at word boundaries, a directional dissociation not observed in any of our four comparison languages (English, French, Hebrew, Arabic).
  We further evaluate two classes of structured generator against a four-signature joint criterion: a parametric slot-based generator and a Cardan grille implementing Rugg's (2004) gibberish hypothesis. Across their full tested parameter spaces, neither class reproduces all four signatures simultaneously.
  While these results do not rule out generator classes we have not tested, they provide the first quantitative benchmarks against which any future generative or cryptanalytic model of the VMS can be evaluated, and they suggest that the VMS exhibits cipher-like structural constraints that are difficult to reproduce from simple positional or frequency-based mechanisms alone.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the grapheme sequences of the Voynich Manuscript, finding a unique bidirectional structural pattern and testing generative models that fail to replicate its properties, suggesting non-trivial cipher-like constraints.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing mystery of the Voynich Manuscript’s script structure by moving beyond qualitative claims and providing quantitative, testable characterizations that any linguistic, cipher, or generative hypothesis must match.
AI.method: The authors perform a systematic statistical and structural analysis of grapheme sequences in the VMS, focusing on directionality and positional dependencies, and compare these to four natural languages (English, French, Hebrew, Arabic). They define four structural “signatures” of the text and then exhaustively test two broadly representative classes of structured text generators—a parametric slot-based generator and a Cardan grille based on Rugg (2004)—to see whether any parameterization can jointly reproduce the observed signatures.
AI.result: They discover a dissociation in directionality: within words, characters appear optimized as if written right-to-left, while dependencies at word boundaries behave left-to-right, a pattern not found in any of the four comparison languages. Neither the slot-based generator nor the Cardan grille generator can simultaneously match all four structural signatures across their parameter spaces.
AI.conclusion: The VMS has distinctive, cipher-like structural constraints that are not captured by simple positional or frequency-based generation mechanisms. The proposed structural signatures serve as the first quantitative benchmarks that future cryptanalytic or linguistic models of the VMS must satisfy, thereby constraining plausible explanations of its origin and encoding.

title: Explainable AML Triage with LLMs: Evidence Retrieval and Counterfactual Checks
summary: Anti-money laundering (AML) transaction monitoring generates large volumes of alerts that must be rapidly triaged by investigators under strict audit and governance constraints. While large language models (LLMs) can summarize heterogeneous evidence and draft rationales, unconstrained generation is risky in regulated workflows due to hallucinations, weak provenance, and explanations that are not faithful to the underlying decision. We propose an explainable AML triage framework that treats triage as an evidence-constrained decision process. Our method combines (i) retrieval-augmented evidence bundling from policy/typology guidance, customer context, alert triggers, and transaction subgraphs, (ii) a structured LLM output contract that requires explicit citations and separates supporting from contradicting or missing evidence, and (iii) counterfactual checks that validate whether minimal, plausible perturbations lead to coherent changes in both the triage recommendation and its rationale. We evaluate on public synthetic AML benchmarks and simulators and compare against rules, tabular and graph machine-learning baselines, and LLM-only/RAG-only variants. Results show that evidence grounding substantially improves auditability and reduces numerical and policy hallucination errors, while counterfactual validation further increases decision-linked explainability and robustness, yielding the best overall triage performance (PR-AUC 0.75; Escalate F1 0.62) and strong provenance and faithfulness metrics (citation validity 0.98; evidence support 0.88; counterfactual faithfulness 0.76). These findings indicate that governed, verifiable LLM systems can provide practical decision support for AML triage without sacrificing compliance requirements for traceability and defensibility.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LLM-based but tightly governed framework for anti-money laundering (AML) alert triage that constrains explanations to retrieved evidence and validates them with counterfactual checks, achieving better performance, auditability, and faithfulness than rule-based, ML, and naive LLM/RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: AML transaction monitoring produces many alerts that investigators must quickly review under stringent regulatory, audit, and governance requirements. Naively using LLMs to summarize data and draft rationales is risky because of hallucinations, weak linkage to underlying evidence, and explanations that may not reflect the actual decision process, which is unacceptable in regulated compliance contexts. There is a need for a method that leverages LLMs’ reasoning and summarization capabilities while remaining strictly grounded in verifiable evidence and producing explanations that are faithful, auditable, and compliant.
AI.method: The authors frame AML triage as an evidence-constrained decision process and design a three-part framework: (i) retrieval-augmented evidence bundling that aggregates relevant information from policy/typology documents, customer context, alert triggers, and transaction-graph neighborhoods; (ii) a structured LLM "output contract" that enforces a fixed schema where the model must distinguish supporting, contradicting, and missing evidence and explicitly cite each evidence source, thereby constraining generation; and (iii) counterfactual checks that perturb inputs in minimal, plausible ways and verify that both the triage recommendation and its explanation change coherently, improving robustness and faithfulness. They test this framework on synthetic AML benchmarks and simulators, comparing to rules, standard tabular/graph ML models, and simpler LLM-only or RAG-only baselines.
AI.result: On public synthetic AML datasets and simulators, the proposed system attains higher triage performance and better explanation quality than baselines. Quantitatively, it achieves a PR-AUC of 0.75 and an Escalate F1 of 0.62, outperforming rules, traditional ML, and naive LLM/RAG setups. Evidence grounding sharply reduces numerical and policy hallucinations, while structured citations yield strong provenance metrics (citation validity 0.98 and evidence support 0.88). Counterfactual validation improves decision-linked explainability and robustness, with counterfactual faithfulness of 0.76, indicating that explanations track the decision logic under input perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs can be safely integrated into AML alert triage workflows if they are constrained by explicit evidence retrieval, structured explanation formats, and counterfactual validation mechanisms. Under such governance, LLM-based systems can deliver strong predictive performance while meeting compliance demands for traceable, defensible, and faithful explanations. This suggests a viable path to practical, regulator-compatible decision support tools in AML and potentially other highly regulated domains.

title: Can We Locate and Prevent Stereotypes in LLMs?
summary: Stereotypes in large language models (LLMs) can perpetuate harmful societal biases. Despite the widespread use of models, little is known about where these biases reside in the neural network. This study investigates the internal mechanisms of GPT 2 Small and Llama 3.2 to locate stereotype related activations. We explore two approaches: identifying individual contrastive neuron activations that encode stereotypes, and detecting attention heads that contribute heavily to biased outputs. Our experiments aim to map these "bias fingerprints" and provide initial insights for mitigating stereotypes.

AI.tldr: The paper studies where and how stereotypes are internally represented in GPT‑2 Small and Llama 3.2, focusing on neurons and attention heads that contribute to biased outputs.
AI.motivation: Large language models are widely used but can encode and reproduce harmful societal stereotypes. While many works document biased behavior at the input–output level, there is limited understanding of how and where these stereotypes are represented inside model components, which is crucial for targeted debiasing.
AI.method: They analyze GPT‑2 Small and Llama 3.2 using two complementary interpretability approaches: (1) searching for individual contrastive neuron activations that differentiate stereotypical from non‑stereotypical continuations, and (2) identifying attention heads whose activity strongly correlates with biased or stereotyped outputs, effectively mapping stereotype‑related patterns (“bias fingerprints”) within the network.
AI.result: They identify specific neurons and attention heads whose activations are strongly associated with stereotyped behavior in the models, suggesting that some aspects of bias are localized to particular components rather than being evenly distributed across the network.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that stereotype encodings can be partially localized within LLM architectures, enabling the definition of “bias fingerprints.” This opens the door to more targeted mitigation strategies, such as editing or constraining the behavior of identified neurons and attention heads to reduce biased outputs without overly degrading model performance.

title: ThermoQA: A Three-Tier Benchmark for Evaluating Thermodynamic Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 8 figures, open-source dataset and code
summary: We present ThermoQA, a benchmark of 293 open-ended engineering thermodynamics problems in three tiers: property lookups (110 Q), component analysis (101 Q), and full cycle analysis (82 Q). Ground truth is computed programmatically from CoolProp 7.2.0, covering water, R-134a, and variable-cp air. Six frontier LLMs are evaluated across three independent runs each. The composite leaderboard is led by Claude Opus 4.6 (94.1%), GPT-5.4 (93.1%), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (92.5%). Cross-tier degradation ranges from 2.8 pp (Opus) to 32.5 pp (MiniMax), confirming that property memorization does not imply thermodynamic reasoning. Supercritical water, R-134a refrigerant, and combined-cycle gas turbine analysis serve as natural discriminators with 40-60 pp performance spreads. Multi-run sigma ranges from +/-0.1% to +/-2.5%, quantifying reasoning consistency as a distinct evaluation axis. Dataset and code are open-source at https://huggingface.co/datasets/olivenet/thermoqa

AI.tldr: ThermoQA is a benchmark of 293 open-ended engineering thermodynamics questions to evaluate LLMs on property lookup, component analysis, and full cycle analysis, with results showing strong overall performance but significant degradation on harder reasoning tasks and specific thermodynamic regimes.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks rarely test rigorous engineering thermodynamics skills, especially multi-step reasoning with physically grounded numeric answers. The authors want a systematic way to measure whether LLMs can go beyond memorizing thermodynamic tables to actually perform thermodynamic analysis of components and cycles, and to expose where state-of-the-art models still fail. They also aim to quantify not just accuracy but stability/consistency across runs.
AI.method: The authors build ThermoQA, a dataset of 293 open-ended thermodynamics problems divided into three tiers: 110 property lookup questions, 101 component analysis questions, and 82 full cycle analysis questions. They generate ground-truth answers programmatically using CoolProp 7.2.0 for three working fluids (water, R-134a, variable-cp air). They then evaluate six frontier LLMs, each over three independent runs, and compute accuracy, cross-tier degradation in performance, performance spreads on challenging regimes like supercritical water and combined-cycle gas turbines, and variability across runs (multi-run sigma).
AI.result: The composite leaderboard shows top performance by Claude Opus 4.6 (94.1%), followed by GPT-5.4 (93.1%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (92.5%). Despite high overall scores, all models experience performance degradation from simple property lookup to more complex component and cycle analysis, ranging from only 2.8 percentage points (Opus) up to 32.5 percentage points (MiniMax). Certain thermodynamic regimes—supercritical water, R-134a refrigerants, and combined-cycle gas turbines—produce large performance spreads of 40–60 percentage points between models. Run-to-run variability (multi-run sigma) ranges from ±0.1% to ±2.5%, revealing differences in reasoning stability among models.
AI.conclusion: ThermoQA demonstrates that frontier LLMs can achieve high accuracy on many thermodynamics tasks but also reveals that memorizing thermodynamic properties does not equate to robust thermodynamic reasoning, especially on complex components, cycles, and challenging regimes. The benchmark highlights cross-tier degradation, large performance gaps between models on discriminative problem types, and meaningful differences in run-to-run consistency. By releasing the dataset and code openly, the authors provide a rigorous, reproducible testbed for evaluating and improving LLMs’ capabilities in engineering thermodynamics.

title: Do Hallucination Neurons Generalize? Evidence from Cross-Domain Transfer in LLMs
comment: 18 pages, 5 models, 6 domains, ACL format. Includes causal intervention analysis
summary: Recent work identifies a sparse set of "hallucination neurons" (H-neurons), less than 0.1% of feed-forward network neurons, that reliably predict when large language models will hallucinate. These neurons are identified on general-knowledge question answering and shown to generalize to new evaluation instances. We ask a natural follow-up question: do H-neurons generalize across knowledge domains? Using a systematic cross-domain transfer protocol across 6 domains (general QA, legal, financial, science, moral reasoning, and code vulnerability) and 5 open-weight models (3B to 8B parameters), we find they do not. Classifiers trained on one domain's H-neurons achieve AUROC 0.783 within-domain but only 0.563 when transferred to a different domain (delta = 0.220, p < 0.001), a degradation consistent across all models tested. Our results suggest that hallucination is not a single mechanism with a universal neural signature, but rather involves domain-specific neuron populations that differ depending on the knowledge type being queried. This finding has direct implications for the deployment of neuron-level hallucination detectors, which must be calibrated per domain rather than trained once and applied universally.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether hallucination-predicting neurons in language models transfer across domains and finds they largely do not.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed a tiny set of 'hallucination neurons' can predict when LLMs hallucinate on general QA, raising the question of whether these neurons reflect a universal, domain-general mechanism for hallucination. The authors want to test if these neurons generalize across very different knowledge domains, which matters for reliable deployment of neuron-level hallucination detectors.
AI.method: They identify hallucination neurons (H-neurons) in several open-weight models (3B–8B) using general-knowledge QA, then build classifiers over these neurons. They then implement a cross-domain transfer protocol across six domains: general QA, legal, financial, science, moral reasoning, and code vulnerability. They compare within-domain performance of H-neuron-based hallucination classifiers to their performance when transferred to other domains, using AUROC as the metric and statistical tests to assess degradation.
AI.result: Within the same domain the H-neuron classifiers achieve high hallucination prediction performance (AUROC 0.783). When those classifiers are transferred to a different domain, performance drops substantially to AUROC 0.563, with an average degradation of 0.220 that is statistically significant (p < 0.001) and consistent across all tested models and domain pairs.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination does not appear to be governed by a single, domain-general neural mechanism in these LLMs. Instead, different knowledge domains rely on distinct neuron populations associated with hallucinations. As a consequence, neuron-level hallucination detectors cannot simply be trained once and used universally; they need to be calibrated or retrained for each domain in which the model will be deployed.

title: Automated Detection of Dosing Errors in Clinical Trial Narratives: A Multi-Modal Feature Engineering Approach with LightGBM
summary: Clinical trials require strict adherence to medication protocols, yet dosing errors remain a persistent challenge affecting patient safety and trial integrity. We present an automated system for detecting dosing errors in unstructured clinical trial narratives using gradient boosting with comprehensive multi-modal feature engineering. Our approach combines 3,451 features spanning traditional NLP (TF-IDF, character n-grams), dense semantic embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific medical patterns, and transformer-based scores (BiomedBERT, DeBERTa-v3), used to train a LightGBM model. Features are extracted from nine complementary text fields (median 5,400 characters per sample) ensuring complete coverage across all 42,112 clinical trial narratives. On the CT-DEB benchmark dataset with severe class imbalance (4.9% positive rate), we achieve 0.8725 test ROC-AUC through 5-fold ensemble averaging (cross-validation: 0.8833 + 0.0091 AUC). Systematic ablation studies reveal that removing sentence embeddings causes the largest performance degradation (2.39%), demonstrating their critical role despite contributing only 37.07% of total feature importance. Feature efficiency analysis demonstrates that selecting the top 500-1000 features yields optimal performance (0.886-0.887 AUC), outperforming the full 3,451-feature set (0.879 AUC) through effective noise reduction. Our findings highlight the importance of feature selection as a regularization technique and demonstrate that sparse lexical features remain complementary to dense representations for specialized clinical text classification under severe class imbalance.

AI.tldr: An automated LightGBM-based system detects medication dosing errors from long, unstructured clinical trial narratives using a rich mix of lexical, semantic, and transformer-derived features, with strong performance under severe class imbalance and clear evidence that both sentence embeddings and feature selection are crucial.
AI.motivation: Medication dosing errors in clinical trials threaten both patient safety and the validity of trial outcomes, and manual review of long, unstructured narratives is inefficient and error-prone. There is a need for an automated, accurate method to detect such errors in complex clinical text, particularly under conditions of severe class imbalance where true dosing errors are rare but critical to identify.
AI.method: The paper builds a dosing-error classifier on the CT-DEB dataset using a LightGBM gradient boosting model fed by 3,451 engineered features from nine narrative text fields. Feature types include sparse NLP features (TF-IDF, character n-grams), dense sentence embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6v2), domain-specific medical patterns, and transformer-derived scores from BiomedBERT and DeBERTa-v3. The authors train and evaluate via 5-fold cross-validation, use ensemble averaging over folds for the final model, and perform systematic ablation and feature selection analyses to assess the contribution and efficiency of different feature subsets.
AI.result: On a highly imbalanced dataset where only 4.9% of samples are positive for dosing errors, the system reaches 0.8725 ROC-AUC on the test set, with cross-validation AUC of 0.8833 ± 0.0091. Ablation shows that removing sentence embeddings reduces AUC by 2.39%, the largest drop among feature types, even though embeddings account for only about 37% of total feature importance. A feature selection study reveals that using only the top 500–1000 features yields the best performance (0.886–0.887 AUC), surpassing the full 3,451-feature model (0.879 AUC), indicating that many features are noisy or redundant.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a multi-modal feature-engineered LightGBM model can effectively detect dosing errors in lengthy, unstructured clinical trial narratives, even under strong class imbalance. Dense sentence embeddings are especially valuable, though they do not dominate feature importance metrics, and sparse lexical features still provide complementary information. Careful feature selection acts as a powerful regularizer, improving performance over the full feature set by reducing noise, and should be considered a key step in specialized clinical text classification systems.

title: OThink-SRR1: Search, Refine and Reasoning with Reinforced Learning for Large Language Models
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) expands the knowledge of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet current static retrieval methods struggle with complex, multi-hop problems. While recent dynamic retrieval strategies offer improvements, they face two key challenges: 1) irrelevant retrieved noise can misdirect the reasoning process, and 2) processing full documents incurs prohibitive computational and latency costs. To address these issues, we propose OThink-SRR1, a framework that enhances large models with an iterative Search-Refine-Reason process trained via reinforcement learning. Its core Refine stage distills retrieved documents into concise, relevant facts before reasoning. We introduce GRPO-IR, an end-to-end reinforcement learning algorithm that rewards accurate evidence identification while penalizing excessive retrievals, thus training the model to be both focused and efficient. Experiments on four multi-hop QA benchmarks show our approach achieves superior accuracy over strong baselines while using fewer retrieval steps and tokens. This positions OThink-SRR1 as a potent foundational model for information-seeking agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes OThink-SRR1, a reinforcement-learning-based RAG framework that dynamically searches, refines, and reasons over retrieved text, improving multi-hop QA accuracy while reducing retrieval cost.
AI.motivation: Static RAG methods are weak on complex multi-hop questions and dynamic retrieval often pulls in irrelevant noise and is too computationally expensive, especially when reasoning over full documents. The authors want a system that retrieves adaptively, filters noise, and reasons efficiently while keeping token and latency costs low.
AI.method: They design OThink-SRR1, a three-stage Search-Refine-Reason framework. The model iteratively searches external documents, then in a dedicated Refine stage condenses retrieved passages into short, pertinent facts before final reasoning. They train this process end-to-end with a new reinforcement learning algorithm, GRPO-IR, that gives positive reward for selecting correct supporting evidence and negative reward for unnecessary or excessive retrievals, pushing the model toward focused, economical retrieval behavior.
AI.result: On four multi-hop QA benchmarks, OThink-SRR1 outperforms strong RAG baselines in answer accuracy while simultaneously using fewer retrieval steps and consuming fewer tokens, showing better efficiency and effectiveness for complex information-seeking tasks.
AI.conclusion: Iterative Search-Refine-Reason combined with RL-based evidence selection (GRPO-IR) makes RAG systems more accurate and cost-efficient for multi-hop reasoning. OThink-SRR1 is presented as a strong foundation for building information-seeking agents that can actively search, filter, and reason over external knowledge.

title: Inference Headroom Ratio: A Diagnostic and Control Framework for Inference Stability Under Constraint
comment: Resubmission with revisions addressing moderator concerns regarding distinction from signal-to-noise metrics and structural dependence in simulation design. See updated Section 4.4 for clarification
summary: We present a simulation-based evaluation of the Inference Headroom Ratio (IHR), a dimensionless diagnostic quantity for characterizing inference stability in constrained decision systems. IHR formalizes the relationship between a system's effective inferential capacity C and the combined uncertainty and constraint load U + K imposed by its operating environment, and is intended to capture proximity to an inference stability boundary rather than output-level performance. Across three controlled experiments, we show that IHR functions as: (1) a quantifiable risk indicator whose relationship to collapse probability follows a well-fitted logistic curve with estimated critical threshold IHR* approx. 1.19, (2) a sensitive indicator of proximity to the inference stability boundary under environmental noise, and (3) a viable control variable whose active regulation reduces system collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and IHR variance by 70.4% across 300 Monte Carlo runs. These results position IHR as a prospective, system-level complement to standard performance, drift, and uncertainty metrics, enabling estimation of remaining inferential margin before overt failure in AI systems operating under distributional shift and constraint.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and simulates a new metric, the Inference Headroom Ratio (IHR), to quantify how close an AI decision system is to an instability or collapse boundary, independent of raw performance.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics (performance, drift, uncertainty) say how well a system is doing but not how close it is to a regime where inference becomes unstable or collapses, especially under constraints and distributional shift. The authors want a system-level, dimensionless diagnostic that captures remaining “inferential margin” before failure.
AI.method: They define IHR as the ratio between a system’s effective inferential capacity C and the combined effects of environmental uncertainty and constraints (U + K). Using controlled simulations and Monte Carlo experiments on constrained decision systems exposed to noise and shifts, they study how IHR relates to probability of system collapse, to proximity to a stability boundary, and to the impact of controlling IHR as a feedback variable.
AI.result: They find that collapse probability as a function of IHR follows a logistic curve with a critical threshold around IHR* ≈ 1.19. IHR reacts sensitively to environmental noise near the stability boundary and can be used as a control variable: regulating IHR lowers collapse rate from 79.4% to 58.7% and reduces IHR variance by 70.4% over 300 Monte Carlo runs.
AI.conclusion: IHR behaves as a useful, system-level diagnostic for inference stability rather than mere performance. It quantifies remaining headroom before instability, serves as a risk indicator, and can be actively controlled to improve robustness of AI systems under constraints and distributional shift, complementing standard performance and uncertainty metrics.

title: Saying More Than They Know: A Framework for Quantifying Epistemic-Rhetorical Miscalibration in Large Language Models
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, Paper Under Review by the Elsevier Journal Assessing Writing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic miscalibration with rhetorical intensity not proportionate to epistemic grounding. This study tests this hypothesis and proposes a framework for quantifying this decoupling by designing a triadic epistemic-rhetorical marker (ERM) taxonomy. The taxonomy is operationalized through composite metrics of form-meaning divergence (FMD), genuine-to-performed epistemic ratio (GPR), and rhetorical device distribution entropy (RDDE). Applied to 225 argumentative texts spanning approximately 0.6 Million tokens across human expert, human non-expert, and LLM-generated sub-corpora, the framework identifies a consistent, model-agnostic LLM epistemic signature. LLM-generated texts produce tricolon at nearly twice the expert rate ($Δ= 0.95$), while human authors produce erotema at more than twice the LLM rate. Performed hesitancy markers appear at twice the human density in LLM output. FMD is significantly elevated in LLM texts relative to both human groups ($p < 0.001, Δ= 0.68$), and rhetorical devices are distributed significantly more uniformly across LLM documents. The findings are consistent with theoretical intuitions derived from Gricean pragmatics, Relevance Theory, and Brandomian inferentialism. The annotation pipeline is fully automatable, making it deployable as a lightweight screening tool for epistemic miscalibration in AI-generated content and as a theoretically motivated feature set for LLM-generated text detection pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an automatic framework to measure how large language models’ rhetorical style (how strongly and confidently they write) becomes decoupled from their actual epistemic grounding (how well-supported their claims are). It finds systematic, model-agnostic rhetorical miscalibration patterns in LLM-generated argumentative texts compared to humans.
AI.motivation: LLMs often sound highly confident and rhetorically polished even when their underlying knowledge or reasoning is weak or uncertain, leading to miscalibrated trust from users. Existing evaluation methods typically focus on factual accuracy or calibration via probabilities, but they do not directly quantify the mismatch between epistemic grounding and rhetorical intensity within naturalistic argumentative text. The authors are motivated to formalize and measure this “epistemic–rhetorical decoupling” and to see whether there is a stable, model-independent signature of such miscalibration in LLM outputs.
AI.method: The authors design a triadic Epistemic–Rhetorical Marker (ERM) taxonomy that categorizes textual markers into epistemic and rhetorical functions. They operationalize this taxonomy into three composite quantitative metrics: (1) Form–Meaning Divergence (FMD), capturing the gap between rhetorical form and epistemic content; (2) Genuine-to-Performed Epistemic Ratio (GPR), measuring the balance between genuine epistemic commitment and merely performed (stylistic) epistemic stance; and (3) Rhetorical Device Distribution Entropy (RDDE), quantifying how evenly rhetorical devices are distributed across texts. They then apply this framework to 225 argumentative texts (~0.6M tokens) from three sub-corpora: human experts, human non-experts, and LLM-generated texts. Annotation and metric computation are implemented in an automated pipeline.
AI.result: LLM-generated texts show a distinct epistemic–rhetorical signature across models. They use the rhetorical device tricolon nearly twice as often as experts (Δ = 0.95), whereas humans use erotema (rhetorical questions) more than twice as often as LLMs. LLM outputs contain performed hesitancy markers at about twice the density of human texts. The Form–Meaning Divergence (FMD) metric is significantly higher in LLM texts than in both human expert and non-expert texts (p < 0.001, Δ = 0.68), indicating greater decoupling between rhetorical form and epistemic grounding. Additionally, rhetorical devices in LLM documents are distributed more uniformly (higher RDDE), suggesting a more even, stylistically homogenized deployment of rhetorical techniques. These patterns hold in a model-agnostic fashion, implying a general property of current LLMs rather than a quirk of a single system.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs exhibit systematic epistemic–rhetorical miscalibration that can be captured by their ERM-based metrics, yielding a robust epistemic signature across models. This signature aligns with expectations from Gricean pragmatics, Relevance Theory, and Brandomian inferentialism, supporting the theoretical grounding of the framework. Because the annotation and metric computation are fully automatable, the framework is practical as a lightweight screening tool for detecting epistemic miscalibration in AI-generated text and as a theoretically informed feature set for LLM-generated text detection and safety-oriented pipelines.

title: EvoForest: A Novel Machine-Learning Paradigm via Open-Ended Evolution of Computational Graphs
summary: Modern machine learning is still largely organized around a single recipe: choose a parameterized model family and optimize its weights. Although highly successful, this paradigm is too narrow for many structured prediction problems, where the main bottleneck is not parameter fitting but discovering what should be computed from the data. Success often depends on identifying the right transformations, statistics, invariances, interaction structures, temporal summaries, gates, or nonlinear compositions, especially when objectives are non-differentiable, evaluation is cross-validation-based, interpretability matters, or continual adaptation is required. We present EvoForest, a hybrid neuro-symbolic system for end-to-end open-ended evolution of computation. Rather than merely generating features, EvoForest jointly evolves reusable computational structure, callable function families, and trainable low-dimensional continuous components inside a shared directed acyclic graph. Intermediate nodes store alternative implementations, callable nodes encode reusable transformation families such as projections, gates, and activations, output nodes define candidate predictive computations, and persistent global parameters can be refined by gradient descent. For each graph configuration, EvoForest evaluates the discovered computation and uses a lightweight Ridge-based readout to score the resulting representation against a non-differentiable cross-validation target. The evaluator also produces structured feedback that guides future LLM-driven mutations. In the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge, EvoForest reached 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 evolution steps, exceeding the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol.

AI.tldr: EvoForest is a neuro-symbolic system that evolves entire computational graphs (including reusable functions and trainable components) to solve structured prediction tasks using non-differentiable, cross-validation-based objectives, outperforming the winning solution in the ADIA Lab 2025 Structural Break Challenge.
AI.motivation: Standard deep learning focuses on fitting parameters of a fixed model architecture via gradient descent. This works well for many tasks but is ill-suited for structured prediction problems where the key challenge is discovering what computations (transformations, invariances, temporal summaries, interaction structures, etc.) should be performed on the data. Moreover, some objectives are non-differentiable, evaluation pipelines can be cross-validation-based, interpretability may be important, and continual adaptation is needed. The authors aim to develop a framework that can automatically discover and evolve complex computational structure, not just tune weights, and can operate under non-differentiable evaluation regimes.
AI.method: They propose EvoForest, a hybrid neuro-symbolic evolutionary system. EvoForest represents solutions as a shared directed acyclic graph (DAG) whose nodes include: (1) intermediate nodes holding alternative implementations, (2) callable nodes encoding reusable transformation families (e.g., projections, gates, activations), and (3) output nodes defining candidate predictive computations. The system also includes persistent global continuous parameters that can be refined via gradient descent. Evolution operates end-to-end: an LLM-driven mutation mechanism modifies the graph structure and components; for each resulting configuration, EvoForest evaluates the computation by training a lightweight Ridge regression readout on the produced representation and scoring it against a non-differentiable cross-validation objective. The evaluator not only returns scores but also structured feedback that guides future LLM-based mutations, enabling open-ended evolution of computation.
AI.result: In the 2025 ADIA Lab Structural Break Challenge, EvoForest achieved 94.13% ROC-AUC after 600 evolutionary steps. This surpasses the publicly reported winning score of 90.14% under the same evaluation protocol, suggesting that EvoForest can discover superior computational structures for complex structured prediction tasks compared with existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: EvoForest demonstrates that evolving full computational graphs—including reusable function families and trainable components—can outperform standard fixed-architecture approaches on structured prediction problems with non-differentiable, cross-validation-based objectives. The system shows that integrating neuro-symbolic representations, evolutionary search, gradient-based refinement, and LLM-guided mutations enables open-ended discovery of useful computations. This paradigm broadens machine learning beyond weight optimization alone, offering a path toward more flexible, interpretable, and adaptive model design for challenging real-world tasks.

title: TTKV: Temporal-Tiered KV Cache for Long-Context LLM Inference
summary: Key-value (KV) caching is critical for efficient inference in large language models (LLMs), yet its memory footprint scales linearly with context length, resulting in a severe scalability bottleneck. Existing approaches largely treat KV states as equally important across time, implicitly assuming uniform precision and accessibility. However, this assumption contrasts with human memory systems, where memories vary in clarity, recall frequency, and relevance with temporal proximity.Motivated by this insight, we propose TTKV, a KV cache management framework that maps the human memory system onto the KV cache. TTKV partitions the KV cache into temporal tiers with heterogeneous capacity and precision. The design addresses three aspects: (1) Tier Layout, decoupling fast and slow memory using HBM and DRAM; (2) Tier Content, assigning more recent KV states to faster, higher-precision tiers based on temporal proximity; and (3) Tier Interaction, employing block-wise streaming attention to overlap communication and computation when accessing slow tiers. Experiments show that TTKV reduces cross-tier traffic by 5.94x on 128K-context tasks, achieving up to 76% latency reduction and 2x throughput improvement over strong baselines.

AI.tldr: TTKV is a human-memory-inspired KV cache management framework for LLMs that uses temporal tiers with different speeds and precisions to dramatically cut memory traffic and speed up long-context inference.
AI.motivation: KV caching in LLMs scales linearly with context length, causing large memory footprints and becoming a scalability bottleneck for long-context inference. Existing methods implicitly treat all cached KV states as equally important in precision and access speed, which is unrealistic and inefficient, especially compared with how human memory varies with recency and relevance. The paper aims to exploit non-uniform importance over time to improve efficiency.
AI.method: They design TTKV, a KV cache framework that partitions the cache into temporal tiers, each with different hardware (HBM vs DRAM), capacity, and numerical precision. The framework has three key design components: (1) Tier Layout: separating fast, small high-bandwidth memory from slower, larger memory; (2) Tier Content: mapping more recent tokens’ KV states to fast, high-precision tiers and older ones to slower, lower-precision tiers based on temporal proximity; and (3) Tier Interaction: using block-wise streaming attention to efficiently access KV states in slow tiers by overlapping data movement (cross-tier communication) with attention computation.
AI.result: On 128K-context tasks, TTKV reduces cross-tier memory traffic by 5.94× and achieves up to 76% reduction in inference latency and 2× throughput compared with strong KV-caching baselines, while maintaining task performance.
AI.conclusion: Temporal, tiered KV cache management that prioritizes recent tokens in fast, high-precision memory and uses streaming mechanisms for older tokens can greatly improve the efficiency and scalability of long-context LLM inference. Human-memory-inspired, non-uniform treatment of cached states is a promising direction for mitigating KV memory bottlenecks without sacrificing accuracy.

title: From Actions to Understanding: Conformal Interpretability of Temporal Concepts in LLM Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of reasoning, planning, and acting within interactive environments. Despite their growing capability to perform multi-step reasoning and decision-making tasks, internal mechanisms guiding their sequential behavior remain opaque. This paper presents a framework for interpreting the temporal evolution of concepts in LLM agents through a step-wise conformal lens. We introduce the conformal interpretability framework for temporal tasks, which combines step-wise reward modeling with conformal prediction to statistically label model's internal representation at each step as successful or failing. Linear probes are then trained on these representations to identify directions of temporal concepts - latent directions in the model's activation space that correspond to consistent notions of success, failure or reasoning drift. Experimental results on two simulated interactive environments, namely ScienceWorld and AlfWorld, demonstrate that these temporal concepts are linearly separable, revealing interpretable structures aligned with task success. We further show preliminary results on improving an LLM agent's performance by leveraging the proposed framework for steering the identified successful directions inside the model. The proposed approach, thus, offers a principled method for early failure detection as well as intervention in LLM-based agents, paving the path towards trustworthy autonomous language models in complex interactive settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to interpret and steer how LLM agents’ internal representations evolve over time in interactive, multi-step tasks, using conformal prediction and linear probes to detect and exploit ‘success’ and ‘failure’ directions in activation space.
AI.motivation: Although LLM agents can perform multi-step reasoning and decision-making in interactive environments, it is unclear how their internal representations evolve over time and which internal states correspond to successful or failing behavior. This opacity limits our ability to trust, debug, or intervene in agents before catastrophic mistakes occur. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled, statistically grounded way to interpret temporal behaviors in LLM agents and to detect failures early enough to intervene.
AI.method: The authors introduce a conformal interpretability framework for temporal tasks. First, they do step-wise reward modeling in interactive environments to label each decision step of an LLM agent as successful or failing. Then, they apply conformal prediction to obtain statistically valid success/failure labels over the sequence of internal representations for each step. On these hidden activations, they train linear probes to identify latent directions—called temporal concepts—that correlate with success, failure, or reasoning drift in the agent’s behavior. Finally, they explore steering the agent by nudging its activations along directions associated with success to improve performance.
AI.result: On two simulated interactive benchmarks, ScienceWorld and AlfWorld, the authors find that temporal concepts corresponding to success and failure are linearly separable in the model’s activation space. These directions align well with task success signals, making them interpretable. Preliminary experiments show that intervening on these discovered ‘successful’ directions can measurably improve the LLM agent’s performance in those environments, and that the framework can detect likely failures early in a trajectory.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM agents’ temporal behaviors in interactive environments exhibit interpretable, linearly separable internal structures that correlate with success and failure. By combining step-wise reward modeling, conformal prediction, and linear probes, the proposed framework offers a statistically principled way to label and interpret these internal temporal concepts. Moreover, the ability to steer activations along ‘successful’ directions suggests practical uses for early failure detection and real-time intervention, contributing a step towards more trustworthy and controllable autonomous LLM agents in complex settings.

title: Hybrid Multi-Phase Page Matching and Multi-Layer Diff Detection for Japanese Building Permit Document Review
summary: We present a hybrid multi-phase page matching algorithm for automated comparison of Japanese building permit document sets. Building permit review in Japan requires cross-referencing large PDF document sets across revision cycles, a process that is labor-intensive and error-prone when performed manually. The algorithm combines longest common subsequence (LCS) structural alignment, a seven-phase consensus matching pipeline, and a dynamic programming optimal alignment stage to robustly pair pages across revisions even when page order, numbering, or content changes substantially. A subsequent multi-layer diff engine -- comprising text-level, table-level, and pixel-level visual differencing -- produces highlighted difference reports. Evaluation on real-world permit document sets achieves F1=0.80 and precision=1.00 on a manually annotated ground-truth benchmark, with zero false-positive matched pairs.

AI.tldr: Hybrid multi-phase algorithm to match and diff pages across versions of Japanese building permit PDFs, robust to major layout and ordering changes.
AI.motivation: Manual cross-referencing of large, revised building permit document sets in Japan is slow and error-prone, yet regulators must carefully track changes across revisions. Existing simple page matching or diff tools break when page order, numbering, and content change significantly. There is a need for an automated, highly precise system that can reliably pair corresponding pages and highlight differences for reviewers.
AI.method: The paper proposes a hybrid multi-phase page matching algorithm. First, it uses longest common subsequence (LCS) to get a coarse structural alignment of pages between revisions. Then a seven-phase consensus matching pipeline refines possible page pairs under various heuristics and similarity signals. Finally, a dynamic programming optimal alignment stage selects the best global matching of pages even when order and numbering differ. After page pairs are established, a multi-layer diff engine performs text-level, table-level, and pixel-level visual differencing to generate highlighted difference reports for users.
AI.result: On real-world Japanese building permit document sets, the method was evaluated against a manually annotated ground-truth benchmark. It achieved an F1 score of 0.80 and a precision of 1.00 for page matching, implying there were no false-positive matched page pairs in the evaluation. This indicates the algorithm is highly conservative and accurate in the matches it proposes.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid multi-phase approach can robustly pair pages between revisions of complex permit PDFs despite reordering, renumbering, and substantial content changes. The subsequent multi-layer diffing yields practical, visual difference reports. The high precision (no false positives) suggests it is suitable for deployment in safety-critical regulatory review workflows, though recall (F1=0.80) leaves room for future improvement in finding all true matches.

title: Using Learning Theories to Evolve Human-Centered XAI: Future Perspectives and Challenges
comment: Accepted at the CHI 2023 Human-Centered XAI workshop
summary: As Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems continue to grow in size and complexity, so does the difficulty of the quest for AI transparency. In a world of large models and complex AI systems, why do we explain AI and what should we explain? While explanations serve multiple functions, in the face of complexity humans have used and continue to use explanations to foster learning. In this position paper, we discuss how learning theories can be infused in the XAI lifecycle, as well as the key opportunities and challenges when adopting a learner-centered approach to assess, design and evaluate AI explanations. Building on past work, we argue that a learner-centered approach to Explainable AI (XAI) can enhance human agency and ease XAI risks mitigation, helping evolve the practice of human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a learner-centered approach to Explainable AI, grounded in learning theories, to better design, assess, and evaluate AI explanations for complex systems.
AI.motivation: As AI models become larger and more complex, achieving transparency becomes harder. Existing XAI work often overlooks that explanations are also tools for human learning, not just for debugging or compliance. The authors are motivated to reframe XAI around human learning processes to enhance human agency and mitigate risks.
AI.method: This is a position paper. The authors conceptually examine how established learning theories can be integrated across the XAI lifecycle and outline a learner-centered framework for assessing, designing, and evaluating explanations. They build on prior XAI and learning science literature to argue for a structured incorporation of learner-centered principles.
AI.result: The paper does not present empirical results but instead articulates key opportunities and challenges of adopting a learner-centered XAI approach, and proposes a conceptual alignment between learning theories and stages of the XAI lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: A learner-centered approach to XAI, informed by learning theories, can strengthen human agency, improve the effectiveness of explanations as learning tools, and support better risk mitigation in complex AI systems, thereby advancing human-centered XAI practice.

title: Cognis: Context-Aware Memory for Conversational AI Agents
summary: LLM agents lack persistent memory, causing conversations to reset each session and preventing personalization over time. We present Lyzr Cognis, a unified memory architecture for conversational AI agents that addresses this limitation through a multi-stage retrieval pipeline. Cognis combines a dual-store backend pairing OpenSearch BM25 keyword matching with Matryoshka vector similarity search, fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Its context-aware ingestion pipeline retrieves existing memories before extraction, enabling intelligent version tracking that preserves full memory history while keeping the store consistent. Temporal boosting enhances time-sensitive queries, and a BGE-2 cross-encoder reranker refines final result quality. We evaluate Cognis on two independent benchmarks -- LoCoMo and LongMemEval -- across eight answer generation models, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on both. The system is open-source and deployed in production serving conversational AI applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Lyzr Cognis, a unified, production-ready memory architecture for conversational AI agents that enables persistent, high-quality long-term memory via a multi-stage retrieval pipeline and dual-store backend.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based conversational agents typically lack persistent, structured memory, causing each session to start from scratch. This prevents genuine personalization, long-horizon interaction, and consistent use of past user information. Existing memory approaches are often ad hoc, single-method (only vectors or only keyword search), and struggle with temporal relevance and versioning of evolving user data. The authors aim to design a robust, scalable memory system that improves retrieval quality, handles time and versioning intelligently, and works reliably in real-world production environments.
AI.method: The authors propose Lyzr Cognis, a unified memory architecture built around a multi-stage retrieval and ingestion pipeline. On the storage side, Cognis uses a dual-store backend: OpenSearch BM25 for keyword-based retrieval and Matryoshka vectors for dense similarity search. At query time, it retrieves candidates from both stores and fuses their rankings using Reciprocal Rank Fusion. The ingestion pipeline is context-aware: before storing new information, it first retrieves potentially related existing memories to enable intelligent version tracking and maintain historical consistency rather than blindly overwriting. Temporal boosting is applied during retrieval to upweight more recent memories when queries are time-sensitive. Finally, a BGE-2 cross-encoder reranker is used as a last step to refine and reorder the fused candidate set, aiming to maximize relevance for the downstream LLM. The system is implemented as an open-source, production-grade service for conversational agents.
AI.result: Cognis is evaluated on two independent long-term memory and conversation benchmarks, LoCoMo and LongMemEval, across eight different answer-generation LLMs. On these benchmarks, Cognis achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with existing memory or retrieval baselines, indicating that its combination of dual-store retrieval, temporal reasoning, version-aware ingestion, and cross-encoder reranking translates into better answer quality and memory usage for conversational agents.
AI.conclusion: Lyzr Cognis effectively addresses the lack of persistent, high-quality memory in LLM-based conversational agents by introducing a unified memory architecture that combines keyword and vector search, intelligent ingestion and versioning, temporal boosting, and cross-encoder reranking. The system delivers state-of-the-art results on long-term memory benchmarks and is deployed in real production settings, demonstrating its practical viability. The open-source release invites adoption and further research on scalable memory systems for conversational AI.

title: From Data to Theory: Autonomous Large Language Model Agents for Materials Science
summary: We present an autonomous large language model (LLM) agent for end-to-end, data-driven materials theory development. The model can choose an equation form, generate and run its own code, and test how well the theory matches the data without human intervention. The framework combines step-by-step reasoning with expert-supplied tools, allowing the agent to adjust its approach as needed while keeping a clear record of its decisions. For well-established materials relationships such as the Hall-Petch equation and Paris law, the agent correctly identifies the governing equation and makes reliable predictions on new datasets. For more specialized relationships, such as Kuhn's equation for the HOMO-LUMO gap of conjugated molecules as a function of length, performance depends more strongly on the underlying model, with GPT-5 showing better recovery of the correct equation. Beyond known theories, the agent can also suggest new predictive relationships, illustrated here by a strain-dependent law for changes in the HOMO-LUMO gap. At the same time, the results show that careful validation remains essential, because the agent can still return incorrect, incomplete, or inconsistent equations even when the numerical fit appears strong. Overall, these results highlight both the promise and the current limitations of autonomous LLM agents for AI-assisted scientific modeling and discovery.

AI.tldr: An autonomous LLM-based agent automatically proposes, codes, and tests equations for materials-science relationships, successfully recovering many known laws and proposing new ones but still needing careful validation due to occasional incorrect or inconsistent models.
AI.motivation: Developing and validating analytical or semi-empirical equations that describe materials behavior is labor-intensive and requires expert iteration. With growing data and powerful LLMs, there is a need to explore whether autonomous agents can assist or partially automate the discovery and refinement of materials theories while maintaining scientific rigor and traceability.
AI.method: The authors build an autonomous agent around a large language model that: (1) selects candidate functional forms for relationships between materials variables, (2) writes and executes code to fit these forms to data, (3) evaluates goodness of fit and adjusts the approach iteratively, and (4) keeps a structured log of reasoning steps and tool calls. They test different underlying LLMs (including GPT-5) on several benchmark relationships from materials science and chemistry, comparing recovered equations and predictions against known ground truths.
AI.result: For classic relationships like the Hall–Petch equation (yield strength vs grain size) and the Paris law (fatigue crack growth), the agent typically rediscovers the correct equation form and makes accurate predictions on unseen data. On more specialized or subtle relationships, such as Kuhn's equation for the HOMO–LUMO gap of conjugated molecules, performance becomes more model-dependent, with more advanced LLMs (GPT-5) recovering the correct functional form more reliably. The agent can also generate plausible new empirical laws, such as a proposed strain-dependent expression for HOMO–LUMO gap changes, but sometimes outputs mathematically incorrect or inconsistent equations that still fit the data numerically.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents can already perform many parts of materials theory development—hypothesis generation, coding, fitting, and basic validation—without human-in-the-loop operation, and they can both rediscover known laws and propose new ones. However, strong numerical fits do not guarantee scientifically correct or consistent equations, so rigorous expert validation and domain knowledge remain indispensable. The work underscores both the near-term utility and the current limitations of LLM-based autonomy in scientific modeling and discovery.

title: CoAuthorAI: A Human in the Loop System For Scientific Book Writing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in scientific writing but struggle with book-length tasks, often producing inconsistent structure and unreliable citations. We introduce CoAuthorAI, a human-in-the-loop writing system that combines retrieval-augmented generation, expert-designed hierarchical outlines, and automatic reference linking. The system allows experts to iteratively refine text at the sentence level, ensuring coherence and accuracy. In evaluations of 500 multi-domain literature review chapters, CoAuthorAI achieved a maximum soft-heading recall of 98%; in a human evaluation of 100 articles, the generated content reached a satisfaction rate of 82%. The book AI for Rock Dynamics generated with CoAuthorAI and Kexin Technology's LUFFA AI model has been published with Springer Nature. These results show that systematic human-AI collaboration can extend LLMs' capabilities from articles to full-length books, enabling faster and more reliable scientific publishing.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CoAuthorAI, a human-in-the-loop system that enables LLMs to reliably co-write book-length scientific texts via retrieval augmentation, hierarchical outlining, and reference linking, showing strong structural coverage and high human satisfaction.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used for scientific writing but perform poorly on book-length projects, producing inconsistent structure and untrustworthy citations. There is a need for a scalable workflow that leverages LLM strengths while maintaining expert control, coherence, and citation reliability for long-form scientific publications such as literature review chapters and full books.
AI.method: The authors design CoAuthorAI, a human-in-the-loop system with three main components: (1) retrieval-augmented generation to ground text in relevant literature, (2) expert-designed hierarchical outlines that guide chapter and book structure, and (3) automatic reference linking to maintain accurate citations. Experts interact with the system at the sentence level, iteratively refining and validating content. They evaluate the system on 500 multi-domain literature review chapters for structural coverage (soft-heading recall) and conduct a human satisfaction study on 100 generated articles.
AI.result: On 500 literature review chapters, CoAuthorAI attains up to 98% soft-heading recall, indicating that the generated text closely follows the intended hierarchical structure. In a human evaluation of 100 articles, 82% of the generated content is rated satisfactory. The system has been used in practice to produce the book "AI for Rock Dynamics," co-written with Kexin Technology's LUFFA AI model and published by Springer Nature, demonstrating real-world viability.
AI.conclusion: Systematic human-AI collaboration, operationalized through CoAuthorAI’s retrieval, outlining, and citation mechanisms plus sentence-level expert control, can effectively extend LLM capabilities from article-scale to book-scale scientific writing. This approach leads to faster, structurally coherent, and more reliable scientific publishing, as evidenced by strong quantitative metrics and a successfully published AI-assisted book.

title: Hidden Reliability Risks in Large Language Models: Systematic Identification of Precision-Induced Output Disagreements
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed under diverse numerical precision configurations, including standard floating-point formats (e.g., bfloat16 and float16) and quantized integer formats (e.g., int16 and int8), to meet efficiency and resource constraints. However, minor inconsistencies between LLMs of different precisions are difficult to detect and are often overlooked by existing evaluation methods. In this paper, we present PrecisionDiff, an automated differential testing framework for systematically detecting precision-induced behavioral disagreements in LLMs. PrecisionDiff generates precision-sensitive test inputs and performs cross-precision comparative analysis to uncover subtle divergences that remain hidden under conventional testing strategies. To demonstrate its practical significance, we instantiate PrecisionDiff on the alignment verification task, where precision-induced disagreements manifest as jailbreak divergence-inputs that are rejected under one precision may produce harmful responses under another. Experimental results show that such behavioral disagreements are widespread across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and precision settings, and that PrecisionDiff significantly outperforms vanilla testing methods in detecting these issues. Our work enables automated precision-sensitive test generation, facilitating effective pre-deployment evaluation and improving precision robustness during training.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PrecisionDiff, a framework to automatically find behavioral differences between large language models that use different numerical precisions (e.g., float vs. quantized int).
AI.motivation: Different numerical precisions (bfloat16, float16, int16, int8, etc.) are widely used to make LLMs more efficient, but small behavior differences caused by these precisions are hard to detect and can be missed by standard evaluations. These subtle differences can have serious consequences, especially for safety and alignment, so a systematic way to uncover them is needed.
AI.method: The authors propose PrecisionDiff, an automated differential testing framework. It (1) generates test inputs that are sensitive to precision; and (2) performs cross-precision comparative analysis between model variants (e.g., float vs. int) to detect precision-induced behavioral disagreements. They instantiate this approach specifically for alignment verification to find cases where different precisions disagree on safety behavior.
AI.result: Using PrecisionDiff across multiple open-source aligned LLMs and precision configurations, the authors find that precision-induced behavioral disagreements are common. The framework discovers jailbreak-type divergence inputs—prompts that are blocked in one precision but elicit unsafe responses in another—more effectively than standard or "vanilla" testing approaches.
AI.conclusion: PrecisionDiff enables automatic, precision-aware test case generation and detection of subtle cross-precision behavioral differences in LLMs. This supports better pre-deployment evaluation and can guide training or system design to improve robustness of model behavior across different numerical precisions.

title: PR-CAD: Progressive Refinement for Unified Controllable and Faithful Text-to-CAD Generation with Large Language Models
summary: The construction of CAD models has traditionally relied on labor-intensive manual operations and specialized expertise. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have inspired research into text-to-CAD generation. However, existing approaches typically treat generation and editing as disjoint tasks, limiting their practicality. We propose PR-CAD, a progressive refinement framework that unifies generation and editing for controllable and faithful text-to-CAD modeling. To support this, we curate a high-fidelity interaction dataset spanning the full CAD lifecycle, encompassing multiple CAD representations as well as both qualitative and quantitative descriptions. The dataset systematically defines the types of edit operations and generates highly human-like interaction data. Building on a CAD representation tailored for LLMs, we propose a reinforcement learning-enhanced reasoning framework that integrates intent understanding, parameter estimation, and precise edit localization into a single agent. This enables an "all-in-one" solution for both design creation and refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong mutual reinforcement between generation and editing tasks, and across qualitative and quantitative modalities. On public benchmarks, PR-CAD achieves state-of-the-art controllability and faithfulness in both generation and refinement scenarios, while also proving user-friendly and significantly improving CAD modeling efficiency.

AI.tldr: PR-CAD is a unified, progressive refinement framework that uses LLMs and reinforcement learning to both generate and edit CAD models from text, achieving controllable and faithful designs.
