While 4-bit quantization is widely regarded as an optimal trade-off, reducing the precision to 2-bit usually triggers a catastrophic ``performance cliff.'' It remains unclear whether the underlying mechanisms differ fundamentally. Consequently, we conduct a systematic mechanistic analysis, revealing two qualitatively distinct failure modes: Signal Degradation, where the computational patterns remain intact but information precision is impaired by cumulative error; and Computation Collapse, where key components fail to function, preventing correct information processing and destroying the signal in the early layers. Guided by this diagnosis, we conduct mechanism-aware interventions, demonstrating that targeted, training-free repair can mitigate Signal Degradation, but remains ineffective for Computation Collapse. Our findings provide a systematic diagnostic framework for PTQ failures and suggest that addressing Computation Collapse requires structural reconstruction rather than mere compensation.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why 2-bit post-training quantization of LLMs fails much more severely than 4-bit, identifying two distinct failure modes and showing that only one is fixable without retraining.
AI.motivation: Post-training quantization enables efficient LLM deployment, but going from 4-bit to 2-bit precision leads to a drastic performance cliff whose mechanisms are not well understood. Without mechanistic insight, it is unclear whether better engineering, calibration, or light-weight fixes can make 2-bit PTQ viable, or whether the failure reflects deeper structural issues in how models compute.
AI.method: The authors perform a mechanistic analysis of LLMs under low-bit PTQ. They probe internal activations and computational pathways to characterize how information is processed after quantization. By comparing 4-bit and 2-bit behaviors, they identify two qualitatively different failure patterns: (1) cases where computations remain structurally intact but noise accumulates, and (2) cases where crucial components effectively stop operating. They then design targeted, training-free interventions tailored to these mechanisms and test whether they can restore performance.
AI.result: They discover two distinct failure modes in low-bit PTQ: (1) Signal Degradation, where the model’s computation graph and functional roles are preserved but quantization noise induces cumulative precision loss; and (2) Computation Collapse, where important components (e.g., specific layers or modules) cease to function properly, disrupting information processing at early stages and destroying the signal. Mechanism-aware, training-free repairs can substantially mitigate Signal Degradation but have little to no effect on Computation Collapse.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a diagnostic framework for understanding PTQ failures in LLMs and shows that the catastrophic performance cliff at 2-bit is not just a more severe version of 4-bit noise, but often involves a qualitatively different failure: Computation Collapse. While Signal Degradation can be addressed with targeted, training-free corrections, Computation Collapse likely requires structural changes or reconstruction rather than simple compensation. This implies that making 2-bit PTQ practical will demand rethinking model or quantization design, not only better calibration or minor post-hoc fixes.

title: Resolving space-sharing conflicts in road user interactions through uncertainty reduction: An active inference-based computational model
summary: Understanding how road users resolve space-sharing conflicts is important both for traffic safety and the safe deployment of autonomous vehicles. While existing models have captured specific aspects of such interactions (e.g., explicit communication), a theoretically-grounded computational framework has been lacking. In this paper, we extend a previously developed active inference-based driver behavior model to simulate interactive behavior of two agents. Our model captures three complementary mechanisms for uncertainty reduction in interaction: (i) implicit communication via direct behavioral coupling, (ii) reliance on normative expectations (stop signs, priority rules, etc.), and (iii) explicit communication. In a simplified intersection scenario, we show that normative and explicit communication cues can increase the likelihood of a successful conflict resolution. However, this relies on agents acting as expected. In situations where another agent (intentionally or unintentionally) violates normative expectations or communicates misleading information, reliance on these cues may induce collisions. These findings illustrate how active inference can provide a novel framework for modeling road user interactions which is also applicable in other fields.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an active-inference-based computational model of how two road users resolve spatial conflicts at intersections, integrating implicit behavior, traffic norms, and explicit communication, and shows both benefits and risks of relying on norms and signals.
AI.motivation: To better understand and predict how road users (including future autonomous vehicles) resolve conflicts for road space, existing models are limited because they focus on isolated aspects such as explicit signaling and lack a unified theoretical and computational framework that explains interaction dynamics and uncertainty reduction.
AI.method: The authors extend an existing active inference driver behavior model from single-agent to two-agent interactive scenarios. They design a simplified intersection setting where two agents must resolve a space-sharing conflict, and formalize three mechanisms for reducing uncertainty: (i) implicit communication via direct behavioral coupling (e.g., yielding, accelerating), (ii) reliance on normative expectations (traffic rules like stop signs and right-of-way), and (iii) explicit communication (signals or gestures). They run simulations of different combinations and reliability of these cues to examine their effect on interaction outcomes.
AI.result: In simulations of intersection conflicts, the model shows that when both agents behave according to norms and use truthful explicit communication, conflicts are more often resolved successfully (i.e., no collision, smoother coordination). However, when one agent violates norms or provides misleading explicit communication, the other agent’s reliance on these cues can actually increase collision risk. Thus, normative and explicit signals help only when they are reliable and correctly interpreted.
AI.conclusion: Active inference provides a coherent computational framework for modeling interactive road user behavior that unifies implicit behavioral coupling, traffic norms, and explicit communication as mechanisms for uncertainty reduction. The paper concludes that while normative rules and explicit communication generally support safe conflict resolution, over-reliance on them can be dangerous when other agents act unexpectedly or deceptively. The framework is proposed as a basis not only for understanding human traffic interactions but also for designing safer autonomous vehicle behavior and may generalize to other interactive domains.

title: Depression Risk Assessment in Social Media via Large Language Models
summary: Depression is one of the most prevalent and debilitating mental health conditions worldwide, frequently underdiagnosed and undertreated. The proliferation of social media platforms provides a rich source of naturalistic linguistic signals for the automated monitoring of psychological well-being. In this work, we propose a system based on Large Language Models (LLMs) for depression risk assessment in Reddit posts, through multi-label classification of eight depression-associated emotions and the computation of a weighted severity index. The method is evaluated in a zero-shot setting on the annotated DepressionEmo dataset (~6,000 posts) and applied in-the-wild to 469,692 comments collected from four subreddits over the period 2024-2025. Our best model, gemma3:27b, achieves micro-F1 = 0.75 and macro-F1 = 0.70, results competitive with purpose-built fine-tuned models (BART: micro-F1 = 0.80, macro-F1 = 0.76). The in-the-wild analysis reveals consistent and temporally stable risk profiles across communities, with marked differences between r/depression and r/anxiety. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of a cost-effective, scalable approach for large-scale psychological monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an LLM-based, zero-shot system to assess depression risk in Reddit posts via multi-label emotion classification and a severity index, achieving performance close to fine-tuned baselines and enabling large-scale, low-cost psychological monitoring.
AI.motivation: Depression is widespread yet frequently underdiagnosed and undertreated. Traditional assessment methods are resource-intensive and do not scale to population-level monitoring. Social media text offers continuous, real-world linguistic signals of mental health status, but existing approaches often require labeled data and task-specific fine-tuning, which are costly and inflexible. The authors aim to exploit recent advances in Large Language Models to build a scalable, zero-shot system for depression risk assessment that can operate effectively without task-specific training.
AI.method: The authors design an LLM-based pipeline that performs multi-label classification of eight emotions associated with depression in Reddit posts, then aggregates these emotion labels into a weighted severity index that reflects depression risk. They evaluate several LLMs, including gemma3:27b, in zero-shot mode on the DepressionEmo dataset of about 6,000 annotated posts. After determining the best-performing model, they apply it to nearly 470,000 comments from four mental-health-related subreddits (collected during 2024–2025) to analyze community-level and temporal risk patterns.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM, gemma3:27b, reaches a micro-F1 score of 0.75 and macro-F1 of 0.70 on the DepressionEmo benchmark, which is competitive with specialized fine-tuned models such as BART (micro-F1 0.80, macro-F1 0.76). When deployed on 469,692 Reddit comments, the system produces consistent and temporally stable depression risk profiles across multiple mental-health communities, and it reveals clear differences between r/depression and r/anxiety in their aggregate risk patterns.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that modern LLMs can be used in a zero-shot setting to approximate the performance of fine-tuned models for depression-related emotion recognition, enabling a cost-effective and scalable way to monitor psychological risk at scale using social media data. The stable risk profiles and distinct community patterns obtained in-the-wild support the potential of such systems for large-scale, continuous mental health surveillance, while suggesting that further refinements could close the remaining performance gap with task-specific models.

title: Deconstructing Superintelligence: Identity, Self-Modification and Différance
summary: Self-modification is often taken as constitutive of artificial superintelligence (SI), yet modification is a relative action requiring a supplement outside the operation. When self-modification extends to this supplement, the classical self-referential structure collapses. We formalise this on an associative operator algebra $\mathcal{A}$ with update $\hat{U}$, discrimination $\hat{D}$, and self-representation $\hat{R}$, identifying the supplement with $\mathrm{Comm}(\hat{U})$; an expansion theorem shows that $[\hat{U},\hat{R}]$ decomposes through $[\hat{U},\hat{D}]$, so non-commutation generically propagates. The liar paradox appears as a commutator collapse $[\hat{T},Π_L]=0$, and class $\mathbf{A}$ self-modification realises the same collapse at system scale, yielding a structure coinciding with Priest's inclosure schema and Derrida's diffèrance.

AI.tldr: The paper studies limits of self-modifying superintelligence using operator algebra, showing that full self-modification leads to a collapse structurally akin to the liar paradox and known logical schemas.
AI.motivation: Self-modification is commonly assumed to be central to artificial superintelligence, but it is philosophically and technically unclear what happens when a system not only modifies itself but also the background structures that define modification. The authors want to clarify whether unbounded self-modification is coherent, and how classical self-reference paradoxes manifest in rigorous mathematical models of such systems.
AI.method: They model an intelligent system on an associative operator algebra A with three operators: an update operator U, a discrimination operator D, and a self-representation operator R. They identify the ‘supplement’—the external structure relative to which modification is defined—with the commutant Comm(U). Using operator commutators, they prove an expansion theorem showing that the commutator [U,R] factors through [U,D], demonstrating how non-commutativity (failure of simultaneous definiteness) propagates when the system attempts deeper forms of self-reference and self-modification. They then interpret well-known logical paradoxes, especially the liar paradox, in the same operator language as a collapse of a commutator [T, Π_L] to zero, and analyse a class A of self-modification processes that reproduce this collapse at the level of the whole system.
AI.result: The authors obtain a formal result (the expansion theorem) stating that the non-commutation between the update and self-representation operators decomposes through their non-commutation with the discrimination operator. This means that once certain parts of the system are non-commutative, attempts at self-representation and self-modification will generically inherit this instability. They show that a particular form of self-modification, which they call class A, forces a commutator collapse analogous to the liar paradox: the system’s global self-modification structure yields [T, Π_L] = 0 in the operator model, matching the structure of known paradoxical schemas in logic and philosophy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that fully general self-modification—where a system can alter not only its own state but also the ‘supplement’ that defines modification—undermines the classical self-referential architecture often assumed in accounts of superintelligence. In the authors’ operator-algebraic formalisation, this leads to a collapse structurally equivalent to the liar paradox and to Priest’s inclosure schema and Derrida’s notion of différance. Thus, the ideal of a completely self-grounding, fully self-modifying superintelligence appears to be mathematically and conceptually unstable, suggesting inherent limits on coherent self-modification in advanced AI systems.

title: Commonsense Knowledge with Negation: A Resource to Enhance Negation Understanding
summary: Negation is a common and important semantic feature in natural language, yet Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle when negation is involved in natural language understanding tasks. Commonsense knowledge, on the other hand, despite being a well-studied topic, lacks investigations involving negation. In this work, we show that commonsense knowledge with negation is challenging for models to understand. We present a novel approach to automatically augment existing commonsense knowledge corpora with negation, yielding two new corpora containing over 2M triples with if-then relations. In addition, pre-training LLMs on our corpora benefits negation understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses LLMs’ difficulty with negation in commonsense reasoning by creating large negated commonsense corpora and showing that pre-training on them improves negation understanding.
AI.motivation: Negation is pervasive in language but LLMs perform poorly when it appears in natural language understanding tasks. At the same time, commonsense knowledge resources rarely incorporate negation explicitly. The authors are motivated to systematically study and improve models’ handling of commonsense knowledge that includes negation.
AI.method: They automatically augment existing commonsense knowledge corpora by injecting negation into if-then commonsense triples, generating over 2 million negated and related triples. These new corpora are then used for pre-training Large Language Models, and their impact on negation understanding is empirically evaluated.
AI.result: The augmentation pipeline successfully produces two large-scale corpora of if-then commonsense triples involving negation. Experiments show that LLMs pre-trained on these corpora exhibit better performance on tasks that require understanding negation.
AI.conclusion: Commonsense knowledge involving negation is challenging for LLMs, but targeted data augmentation and pre-training on large negated commonsense corpora can significantly improve models’ negation understanding. The work also provides new resources for further research on negation-aware commonsense reasoning.

title: Learning When Not to Decide: A Framework for Overcoming Factual Presumptuousness in AI Adjudication
summary: A well-known limitation of AI systems is presumptuousness: the tendency of AI systems to provide confident answers when information may be lacking. This challenge is particularly acute in legal applications, where a core task for attorneys, judges, and administrators is to determine whether evidence is sufficient to reach a conclusion. We study this problem in the important setting of unemployment insurance adjudication, which has seen rapid integration of AI systems and where the question of additional fact-finding poses the most significant bottleneck for a system that affects millions of applicants annually. First, through a collaboration with the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment, we secure rare access to official training materials and guidance to design a novel benchmark that systematically varies in information completeness. Second, we evaluate four leading AI platforms and show that standard RAG-based approaches achieve an average of only 15% accuracy when information is insufficient. Third, advanced prompting methods improve accuracy on inconclusive cases but over-correct, withholding decisions even on clear cases. Fourth, we introduce a structured framework requiring explicit identification of missing information before any determination (SPEC, Structured Prompting for Evidence Checklists). SPEC achieves 89% overall accuracy, while appropriately deferring when evidence is insufficient -- demonstrating that presumptuousness in legal AI is systematic but addressable, and that doing so is a necessary step towards systems that reliably support, rather than supplant, human judgment wherever decisions must await sufficient evidence.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses AI presumptuousness in legal settings and proposes a structured prompting framework (SPEC) that significantly reduces incorrect confident answers by forcing explicit reasoning about missing evidence.
AI.motivation: AI systems, especially large language models, often answer confidently even when the available information is insufficient. In legal domains like unemployment insurance adjudication, such presumptuousness is particularly dangerous because decision-makers must know when the record is incomplete and additional fact-finding is required. Existing AI deployments in this area face bottlenecks around determining sufficiency of evidence, and there is a need to rigorously evaluate and improve AI behavior on this dimension.
AI.method: The authors collaborate with the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment to obtain internal training materials and guidance used by human adjudicators. Using this domain-grounded material, they construct a benchmark that systematically varies the completeness of information in unemployment insurance cases. They then evaluate four leading AI platforms, focusing on standard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines and advanced prompting strategies, measuring performance specifically on cases with sufficient vs. insufficient evidence. Finally, they introduce SPEC (Structured Prompting for Evidence Checklists), a prompting framework that requires the model to explicitly list required evidence items, check which are present or missing, and only then make or defer a determination.
AI.result: Standard RAG-based approaches perform poorly on cases with insufficient information, achieving about 15% accuracy when they should withhold judgment, indicating strong presumptuousness. Advanced prompting improves recognition of inconclusive cases but swings too far, leading models to avoid making decisions even when the evidence is clear enough. In contrast, the SPEC structured prompting framework attains 89% overall accuracy and shows appropriate behavior in deferring judgment when the evidence is insufficient.
AI.conclusion: Presumptuousness in legal AI systems is systematic but can be mitigated through carefully designed structured prompting. By forcing explicit identification and evaluation of required evidence before issuing a decision, frameworks like SPEC can substantially improve both accuracy and appropriate deferral, making AI systems more suitable as decision-support tools rather than unsafe substitutes for human judgment in evidence-sensitive domains such as unemployment insurance adjudication.

title: Tracing Relational Knowledge Recall in Large Language Models
summary: We study how large language models recall relational knowledge during text generation, with a focus on identifying latent representations suitable for relation classification via linear probes. Prior work shows how attention heads and MLPs interact to resolve subject, predicate, and object, but it remains unclear which representations support faithful linear relation classification and why some relation types are easier to capture linearly than others. We systematically evaluate different latent representations derived from attention head and MLP contributions, showing that per-head attention contributions to the residual stream are comparatively strong features for linear relation classification. Feature attribution analyses of the trained probes, as well as characteristics of the different relation types, reveal clear correlations between probe accuracy and relation specificity, entity connectedness, and how distributed the signal on which the probe relies is across attention heads. Finally, we show how token-level feature attribution of probe predictions can be used to reveal probe behavior in further detail.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates which internal representations in large language models best support linear classification of relational knowledge, finding that per-head attention contributions are strong features and that probe performance depends on relation properties and how distributed their signals are across heads.
AI.motivation: While prior work has explored how attention heads and MLPs in large language models cooperate to resolve subject, predicate, and object, it is still unclear which specific latent representations can be reliably used for linear relation classification, and why some relation types are more linearly learnable than others. The authors aim to clarify where in the model relational knowledge is stored in a way that is accessible to simple probes, and what characteristics of relations make them easier or harder to capture.
AI.method: The authors construct and compare a range of latent representations derived from attention head outputs and MLP contributions to the residual stream. They train linear probes on these representations to perform relation classification and systematically compare performance across representations. They then apply feature attribution techniques to the trained probes to understand which features (e.g., which heads or tokens) they rely on, and correlate probe accuracy with properties of relation types such as specificity, entity connectedness, and distribution of relevant signals across attention heads. Finally, they use token-level feature attribution for individual probe predictions to analyze probe behavior in more detail.
AI.result: Per-head attention contributions to the residual stream emerge as particularly strong and informative features for linear relation classification, outperforming other tested representations. The authors find that probe accuracy is systematically related to relation specificity, the degree of connectedness between entities, and whether the relevant signal is concentrated in a few heads or spread across many. They also demonstrate that token-level feature attribution can expose which parts of the input and which internal components the probes rely on, giving a nuanced picture of probe decision-making.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that relational knowledge in large language models is encoded in ways that make per-head attention contributions especially suitable for linear probing. The linear learnability of different relation types depends not only on the presence of relational information but also on relation-specific properties and the distribution of signals across heads. Token-level attributions provide a powerful tool for interpreting probe behavior, contributing to a more precise understanding of how and where relational knowledge is represented inside large language models.

title: CreativeGame:Toward Mechanic-Aware Creative Game Generation
summary:
Large language models can generate plausible game code, but turning this capability into \emph{iterative creative improvement} remains difficult. In practice, single-shot generation often produces brittle runtime behavior, weak accumulation of experience across versions, and creativity scores that are too subjective to serve as reliable optimization signals. A further limitation is that mechanics are frequently treated only as post-hoc descriptions, rather than as explicit objects that can be planned, tracked, preserved, and evaluated during generation.
  This report presents \textbf{CreativeGame}, a multi-agent system for iterative HTML5 game generation that addresses these issues through four coupled ideas: a proxy reward centered on programmatic signals rather than pure LLM judgment; lineage-scoped memory for cross-version experience accumulation; runtime validation integrated into both repair and reward; and a mechanic-guided planning loop in which retrieved mechanic knowledge is converted into an explicit mechanic plan before code generation begins. The goal is not merely to produce a playable artifact in one step, but to support interpretable version-to-version evolution.
  The current system contains 71 stored lineages, 88 saved nodes, and a 774-entry global mechanic archive, implemented in 6{,}181 lines of Python together with inspection and visualization tooling. The system is therefore substantial enough to support architectural analysis, reward inspection, and real lineage-level case studies rather than only prompt-level demos.
  A real 4-generation lineage shows that mechanic-level innovation can emerge in later versions and can be inspected directly through version-to-version records. The central contribution is therefore not only game generation, but a concrete pipeline for observing progressive evolution through explicit mechanic change.

AI.tldr: CreativeGame is a multi-agent system that iteratively generates and evolves HTML5 games using explicit mechanic planning, runtime validation, and programmatic rewards, enabling interpretable lineage-level improvement rather than one-shot code generation.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based game generation is typically single-shot: it yields brittle game code, poor reuse of experience across versions, and relies on subjective creativity scores that are unreliable optimization targets. Moreover, game mechanics are usually only described after the fact instead of being explicit, trackable entities in the generation loop. The authors want a system where creativity can be optimized via clearer, programmatic signals, where game ideas and mechanics can evolve over versions, and where this evolution is observable and analyzable.
AI.method: They design CreativeGame, a multi-agent system for HTML5 game generation with four main components: (1) a proxy reward function emphasizing measurable, programmatic signals rather than pure LLM self-evaluation; (2) lineage-scoped memory that stores and reuses information across different versions of the same game concept; (3) runtime validation hooked into both automatic code repair and the reward computation, so that behavior during execution informs iteration; and (4) a mechanic-guided planning loop, where the system retrieves mechanic knowledge from a global archive and converts it into an explicit mechanic plan before generating code. The implementation includes Python infrastructure, mechanic archives, and visualization/inspection tools to analyze lineages and rewards.
AI.result: They implemented CreativeGame with 71 stored game lineages, 88 saved game nodes (versions), and a global archive of 774 mechanics, all in about 6,181 lines of Python plus visualization tooling. This scale allows architectural examination of the system, analysis of the reward signals, and detailed case studies of how individual game lineages evolve over generations, rather than just isolated prompt-response examples.
AI.conclusion: CreativeGame shows that LLM-based game generation can be structured as an interpretable, iterative evolutionary process centered on explicit mechanics. By using programmatic rewards, lineage-aware memory, and runtime validation, the system supports observable mechanic-level innovation in later generations and enables direct inspection of how game mechanics change over time. The contribution is thus both a game generator and an analysis framework for progressive mechanic evolution across versions.

title: Structured Disagreement in Health-Literacy Annotation: Epistemic Stability, Conceptual Difficulty, and Agreement-Stratified Inference
summary: Annotation pipelines in Natural Language Processing (NLP) commonly assume a single latent ground truth per instance and resolve disagreement through label aggregation. Perspectivist approaches challenge this view by treating disagreement as potentially informative rather than erroneous. We present a large-scale analysis of graded health-literacy annotations from 6,323 open-ended COVID-19 responses collected in Ecuador and Peru. Each response was independently labeled by multiple annotators using proportional correctness scores, reflecting the degree to which responses align with normative public-health guidelines, allowing us to analyze the full distribution of judgments rather than aggregated labels. Variance decomposition shows that question-level conceptual difficulty accounts for substantially more variance than annotator identity, indicating that disagreement is structured by the task itself rather than driven by individual raters. Agreement-stratified analyses further reveal that key social-scientific effects, including country, education, and urban-rural differences, vary in magnitude and in some cases reverse direction across levels of inter-annotator agreement. These findings suggest that graded health-literacy evaluation contains both epistemically stable and unstable components, and that aggregating across them can obscure important inferential differences. We therefore argue that strong perspectivist modeling is not only conceptually justified but statistically necessary for valid inference in graded interpretive tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that in graded health-literacy annotation tasks, disagreement among annotators is informative and structured by the task, so perspectivist modeling (retaining distributions of judgments) is necessary for valid inference instead of collapsing labels into a single ground truth.
AI.motivation: Conventional NLP annotation assumes a single correct label per instance and resolves annotator disagreement via aggregation, which can obscure meaningful variation in how people interpret or evaluate content. In graded, interpretive tasks like assessing health literacy, especially during a public health crisis, understanding this variation is crucial for accurate social-scientific inference and policy insights. The authors want to test whether disagreement is due mainly to annotator idiosyncrasies or reflects stable, task-driven uncertainty and heterogeneity in judgments, and whether aggregation distorts key substantive effects (e.g., demographic differences).
AI.method: The authors collect 6,323 open-ended responses about COVID-19 from Ecuador and Peru and have multiple annotators independently score each response with proportional correctness scores that indicate alignment with public-health guidelines. Instead of collapsing labels, they analyze the full distribution of scores per instance. They use variance decomposition to partition variance in annotations into components attributable to question-level difficulty versus annotator identity. They then stratify analyses by levels of inter-annotator agreement and examine how estimated social-scientific effects (e.g., differences by country, education, urban/rural status) change in magnitude or direction across agreement strata, to separate epistemically stable from unstable components of the evaluation task.
AI.result: Variance decomposition shows that question-level conceptual difficulty explains much more variance in annotations than annotator identity, indicating that disagreement is primarily driven by the task’s inherent difficulty or ambiguity rather than random annotator noise. When they analyze demographic and social effects conditional on agreement levels, they find that estimated effects such as country differences, education effects, and urban-rural gaps vary substantially by agreement stratum; in some cases these effects even reverse direction between high- and low-agreement subsets. This demonstrates that what looks like a single effect when labels are aggregated actually bundles together heterogeneous and sometimes conflicting inferential signals.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that graded health-literacy annotation tasks comprise both epistemically stable components (where annotators largely agree) and unstable components (where disagreement is systematic and substantial). Aggregating annotations into a single ground-truth score obscures this structure and can yield misleading inferences about key social-scientific relationships. Therefore, a strong perspectivist approach—explicitly modeling and retaining the distribution of annotator judgments—is not only conceptually appealing but statistically required for valid inference in graded, interpretive NLP tasks.

title: What Makes a Good AI Review? Concern-Level Diagnostics for AI Peer Review
summary: Evaluating AI-generated reviews by verdict agreement is widely recognized as insufficient, yet current alternatives rarely audit which concerns a system identifies, how it prioritizes them, or whether those priorities align with the review rationale that shaped the final assessment. We propose concern alignment, a diagnostic framework that evaluates AI reviews at the concern level rather than only at the verdict level. The framework's core data structure is the match graph, a bipartite alignment between official and AI-generated concerns annotated with match type, severity, and post-rebuttal treatment. From this artifact we derive an evaluation ladder that moves from binary accuracy to concern detection, verdict-stratified behavior, decision-aware calibration, and rebuttal-aware decomposition. In a pilot study of four public AI review systems evaluated in six configurations, concern-level analysis suggests that detection alone does not determine review quality; calibration is often the binding constraint. Systems detect non-trivial fractions of official concerns yet most mark 25--55% of concerns on accepted papers as decisive, where, under our operationalization, no official concern on accepted papers was treated as a decisive blocker. Identical overall verdict accuracy can conceal reject-heavy behavior versus low-recall profiles, and low full-review false decisive rates can partly reflect concern dilution rather than calibrated prioritization. Most systems do not emit a native accept/reject, and inferring it from review tone is method-sensitive, reinforcing the need for concern-level diagnostics that remain stable across inference choices. The contribution is a reusable evaluation framework for auditing which concerns AI reviewers identify, how they weight them, and whether those priorities align with the review rationale that informed the paper's final assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a concern-level evaluation framework for AI-generated peer reviews, going beyond simple verdict agreement, using a match-graph structure to analyze which concerns are raised, how they’re weighted, and how that aligns with final decisions.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of AI-generated peer reviews mostly compare overall accept/reject verdicts against official decisions, which misses whether the AI identifies the right issues, prioritizes them appropriately, or mirrors the reasoning behind final judgments. The authors want a more fine-grained, diagnostic way to audit the behavior and reliability of AI reviewers.
AI.method: They define a diagnostic framework called concern alignment, centered on a bipartite match graph that aligns concerns raised by official human reviews with those raised by AI systems. Each matched pair is annotated with match type, severity, and treatment after rebuttal. From this structure, they derive an evaluation ladder of increasingly detailed metrics: basic accuracy, concern detection rates, behavior stratified by verdict, calibration of concern severity relative to decisions, and how concerns change post-rebuttal. They apply this framework in a pilot study to four public AI review systems under six configurations.
AI.result: Concern-level analysis reveals that mere detection of relevant issues is not the main bottleneck; rather, systems often mis-calibrate the importance of concerns. Across evaluated setups, AI systems identify a substantial portion of official concerns but frequently label 25–55% of concerns on accepted papers as decisive blockers, whereas under the authors’ operationalization no concern on accepted papers was actually decisive. The same overall verdict accuracy can hide qualitatively different behaviors (e.g., reject-heavy vs low-recall patterns), and seemingly low false decisive rates can partly come from diluting concerns. Many systems lack explicit accept/reject outputs, and verdicts inferred from tone vary with the inference method.
AI.conclusion: The authors present a reusable, concern-level evaluation framework that enables nuanced auditing of AI reviewers: what concerns they surface, how they prioritize them, and how that prioritization aligns with human review rationales and final decisions. They argue such concern alignment metrics are necessary because verdict-level accuracy alone is unstable and obscures important differences in behavior and calibration across AI review systems.

title: From Recall to Forgetting: Benchmarking Long-Term Memory for Personalized Agents
summary: Personalized agents that interact with users over long periods must maintain persistent memory across sessions and update it as circumstances change. However, existing benchmarks predominantly frame long-term memory evaluation as fact retrieval from past conversations, providing limited insight into agents' ability to consolidate memory over time or handle frequent knowledge updates. We introduce Memora, a long-term memory benchmark spanning weeks to months long user conversations. The benchmark evaluates three memory-grounded tasks: remembering, reasoning, and recommending. To ensure data quality, we employ automated memory-grounding checks and human evaluation. We further introduce Forgetting-Aware Memory Accuracy (FAMA), a metric that penalizes reliance on obsolete or invalidated memory when evaluating long-term memory. Evaluations of four LLMs and six memory agents reveal frequent reuse of invalid memories and failures to reconcile evolving memories. Memory agents offer marginal improvements, exposing shortcomings in long-term memory for personalized agents.

AI.tldr: Memora is a benchmark for evaluating long-term memory in personalized agents over weeks-to-months conversations, introducing new tasks and a forgetting-aware metric to reveal current systems’ limitations.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks mostly test simple fact retrieval from past dialogue, which doesn’t reflect realistic long-term interaction where user information evolves, becomes obsolete, or must be consolidated. The paper aims to capture these realistic dynamics and properly assess agents’ ability to maintain, update, and use long-term memory over extended periods.
AI.method: The authors build Memora, a benchmark consisting of synthetic but realistic, weeks-to-months-long user–agent conversations. It defines three evaluation tasks—remembering, reasoning, and recommending—that are explicitly grounded in the agent’s memory. They add automated checks to ensure that evaluation items are truly memory-grounded and complement this with human evaluation. They also propose a new metric, Forgetting-Aware Memory Accuracy (FAMA), which scores answers while penalizing the use of outdated or invalidated memories.
AI.result: Using Memora, they evaluate four base LLMs and six memory-augmented agent systems. The experiments show that models frequently reuse invalid memories and struggle to handle evolving or conflicting information. The memory-augmented agents provide only modest gains over base LLMs, and none of the systems robustly manage long-term, changing user information.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs and memory agents are far from solving realistic long-term memory for personalized agents. Benchmarks must account for information decay, updates, and contradictions; Memora and FAMA expose these weaknesses and offer a more faithful testbed for developing better long-term memory mechanisms.

title: Separable Pathways for Causal Reasoning: How Architectural Scaffolding Enables Hypothesis-Space Restructuring in LLM Agents
summary: Causal discovery through experimentation and intervention is fundamental to robust problem solving. It requires not just updating beliefs within a fixed framework but revising the hypothesis space itself, a capacity current AI agents lack when evidence demands representations they have not previously constructed. We extend the blicket detector paradigm from developmental science to test this capacity in AI agents equipped with architectural scaffolding that targets hypothesis-space restructuring. Our compositional architecture has two discrete components: context graphs, which structure exploration as typed state machines, and dynamic behaviors, which monitor for evidence that the current hypothesis space is inadequate and expand it at runtime. Across 1,085 experimental trials, these components make orthogonal contributions: context graphs drive reasoning quality within the post-switch hypothesis space, accounting for 94\% of the accuracy gain, while dynamic behaviors drive reasoning eligibility by detecting regime changes and preventing premature commitment to outdated hypotheses.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a compositional AI architecture that can revise its own hypothesis space during causal discovery, enabling better adaptation to regime changes in experiments.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents can update beliefs within a fixed model but struggle when data require entirely new kinds of hypotheses or representations. Inspired by human causal learning (e.g., blicket detector studies), the authors aim to build agents that can notice when their current causal framework is inadequate and then restructure it, rather than just tweak parameters within it.
AI.method: They extend the blicket detector paradigm from developmental psychology into a benchmark for AI agents. They propose a compositional architecture with two main elements: (1) context graphs, which represent and guide exploration as typed state machines, effectively structuring how the agent explores and reasons in different experimental contexts; and (2) dynamic behaviors, which monitor incoming evidence for signs that the current hypothesis space no longer fits (e.g., regime switches) and, upon detection, dynamically expand or revise the hypothesis space at runtime. They run 1,085 experimental trials to quantify the contributions of each architectural component.
AI.result: The experiments show that the two components contribute in different, largely independent ways. Context graphs mainly improve the quality of reasoning after the agent has moved to an appropriate hypothesis space, explaining about 94% of the total improvement in accuracy. Dynamic behaviors mainly ensure the agent is reasoning in the right hypothesis space at the right time by detecting regime changes and avoiding overcommitment to outdated models. Together, they significantly enhance causal discovery performance in the extended blicket tasks.
AI.conclusion: A compositional architecture that explicitly separates structured exploration (context graphs) from hypothesis-space management (dynamic behaviors) enables AI agents to revise their own hypothesis spaces during causal discovery. This leads to robust adaptation when causal regimes change, with context graphs largely responsible for higher reasoning accuracy and dynamic behaviors for correct detection of when a new hypothesis space is needed. The work suggests that supporting hypothesis-space restructuring, not just belief updating, is key for more human-like, flexible causal reasoning in AI systems.

title: TriEx: A Game-based Tri-View Framework for Explaining Internal Reasoning in Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: Explainability for Large Language Model (LLM) agents is especially challenging in interactive, partially observable settings, where decisions depend on evolving beliefs and other agents. We present \textbf{TriEx}, a tri-view explainability framework that instruments sequential decision making with aligned artifacts: (i) structured first-person self-reasoning bound to an action, (ii) explicit second-person belief states about opponents updated over time, and (iii) third-person oracle audits grounded in environment-derived reference signals. This design turns explanations from free-form narratives into evidence-anchored objects that can be compared and checked across time and perspectives. Using imperfect-information strategic games as a controlled testbed, we show that TriEx enables scalable analysis of explanation faithfulness, belief dynamics, and evaluator reliability, revealing systematic mismatches between what agents say, what they believe, and what they do. Our results highlight explainability as an interaction-dependent property and motivate multi-view, evidence-grounded evaluation for LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/Einsam1819/TriEx.

AI.tldr: TriEx is a tri-view explainability framework for LLM agents in interactive, partially observable environments, aligning self-reasoning, beliefs about others, and oracle audits to make explanations evidence-anchored and evaluable over time.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM explainability struggles in sequential, multi-agent, partially observable settings because explanations are typically free-form, hard to verify, and disconnected from beliefs and actions. The authors want a way to systematically analyze how what an agent says, believes, and does may diverge, in order to better evaluate the faithfulness and reliability of explanations.
AI.method: They design TriEx, which instruments LLM agents with three synchronized artifacts during decision making: (1) first-person structured self-reasoning tied to each chosen action; (2) second-person explicit belief states about other agents, updated over time; and (3) third-person oracle audits based on reference signals from the environment. Using imperfect-information strategic games as a controlled testbed, they log and compare these views over time to study explanation faithfulness, belief dynamics, and evaluator behavior.
AI.result: In game-based experiments, TriEx supports scalable, systematic analysis across the three views, uncovering consistent mismatches between agents' stated explanations, their internal beliefs about opponents, and their observable actions. It also enables measurement of explanation faithfulness and reliability of evaluators, showing that explanations can be misleading or misaligned with behavior.
AI.conclusion: Explainability for LLM agents depends critically on interaction context and cannot be reliably assessed from single-view, free-form narratives. Multi-view, evidence-grounded frameworks like TriEx are needed to capture and evaluate the relationships between self-reported reasoning, beliefs about others, and actual decisions in sequential, partially observable environments.

title: From Fuzzy to Formal: Scaling Hospital Quality Improvement with AI
summary: Hospital Quality Improvement (QI) plays a critical role in optimizing healthcare delivery by translating high-level hospital goals into actionable solutions. A critical step of QI is to identify the key modifiable contributing factors, a process we call QI factor discovery, typically through expert-driven semi-structured qualitative tools like fishbone diagrams, chart reviews, and Lean Healthcare methods. AI has the potential to transform and accelerate QI factor discovery, which is traditionally time- and resource-intensive and limited in reproducibility and auditability. Nevertheless, current AI alignment methods assume the task is well-defined, whereas QI factor discovery is an exploratory, fuzzy, and iterative sense-making process that relies on complex implicit expert judgments. To design an AI pipeline that formalizes the QI process while preserving its exploratory components, we propose viewing the task as learning not only LLM prompts but also the overarching natural-language specifications. In particular, we map QI factor discovery to steps of the classical AI/ML development process (problem formalization, model learning, and model validation) where the specifications are tunable hyperparameters. Domain experts and AI agents iteratively refine both the overarching specifications and AI pipeline until AI extractions are concordant with expert annotations and aligned with clinical objectives. We applied this "Human-AI Spec-Solution Co-optimization" framework at an urban safety-net hospital to identify factors driving prolonged length of stay and unplanned 30-day readmissions. The resulting AI-for-QI pipelines achieved $\ge 70\%$ concordance with expert annotations. Compared to prior manual Lean analyses, the AI pipeline was substantially more efficient, recovered previous findings, surfaced new modifiable factors, and produced auditable reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: AI-assisted framework to discover modifiable factors for hospital quality improvement, co-optimizing human specifications and AI pipelines to efficiently match expert insights.
AI.motivation: Quality improvement factor discovery in hospitals is essential but currently slow, resource-intensive, and hard to reproduce or audit. Existing AI alignment methods assume well-defined tasks, which clashes with the fuzzy, exploratory nature of QI work driven by expert judgment. There is a need for an AI framework that can formalize and support this exploratory process without losing its flexibility, enabling faster, more scalable, and auditable QI analyses.
AI.method: The authors reconceptualize QI factor discovery as a process of jointly learning both natural-language task specifications and LLM-based extraction pipelines. They map QI work onto the classical ML lifecycle: problem formalization, model learning, and validation, but treat the specifications themselves as tunable hyperparameters. Domain experts and AI agents iteratively refine both the specifications and the AI pipeline in a "Human-AI Spec-Solution Co-optimization" loop until AI-driven extractions align with expert annotations and clinical goals. They instantiate this framework in an urban safety-net hospital to analyze factors behind prolonged length of stay and unplanned 30-day readmissions.
AI.result: The AI-for-QI pipelines built under the proposed framework achieved at least 70% concordance with expert annotations. They replicated insights previously obtained via manual Lean analyses, while also uncovering new modifiable contributing factors. The AI approach was substantially more efficient than traditional methods and created transparent, auditable reasoning traces for its outputs.
AI.conclusion: Treating QI factor discovery as a joint optimization over natural-language specifications and AI extraction pipelines enables AI systems to support an inherently exploratory, judgment-heavy process. The proposed Human-AI Spec-Solution Co-optimization framework can produce efficient, reproducible, and auditable QI analyses that meaningfully align with expert assessments and recover as well as extend traditional Lean-based findings.

title: Large language models perceive cities through a culturally uneven baseline
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to describe, evaluate and interpret places, yet it remains unclear whether they do so from a culturally neutral standpoint. Here we test urban perception in frontier LLMs using a balanced global street-view sample and prompts that either remain neutral or invoke different regional cultural standpoints. Across open-ended descriptions and structured place judgments, the neutral condition proved not to be neutral in practice. Prompts associated with Europe and Northern America remained systematically closer to the baseline than many non-Western prompts, indicating that model perception is organized around a culturally uneven reference frame rather than a universal one. Cultural prompting also shifted affective evaluation, producing sentiment-based ingroup preference for some prompted identities. Comparisons with regional human text-image benchmarks showed that culturally proximate prompting could improve alignment with human descriptions, but it did not recover human levels of semantic diversity and often preserved an affectively elevated style. The same asymmetry reappeared in structured judgments of safety, beauty, wealth, liveliness, boredom and depression, where model outputs were interpretable but only partly reproduced human group differences. These findings suggest that LLMs do not simply perceive cities from nowhere: they do so through a culturally uneven baseline that shapes what appears ordinary, familiar and positively valued.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether large language models (LLMs) describe cities from a culturally neutral standpoint and finds they do not.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly applied to tasks involving the description and evaluation of places, it is important to understand whether their outputs reflect a universal perspective or are biased toward certain cultural standpoints, which can impact fairness and representativeness.
AI.method: The authors use a balanced global street-view image dataset and query frontier LLMs with prompts that are either neutral or framed from specific regional cultural standpoints. They analyze both open-ended textual descriptions and structured judgments (e.g., safety, beauty, wealth) and compare model outputs to regional human text-image benchmarks.
AI.result: The supposedly neutral prompting condition aligns more closely with prompts associated with Europe and Northern America than with non-Western prompts, indicating a culturally uneven baseline. Cultural prompting shifts affective evaluations, sometimes producing ingroup sentiment preference. While culturally proximate prompts can improve similarity to regional human descriptions, they fail to match human semantic diversity and maintain a more positively valenced style. Structured judgments show interpretable but only partial reproduction of human group differences.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do not perceive urban scenes from a culturally neutral “view from nowhere”; instead, their outputs reflect a culturally skewed reference frame that privileges certain regions and shapes notions of what is normal, familiar, and positively valued, limiting their validity as universal urban perception tools.

title: EvoAgent: An Evolvable Agent Framework with Skill Learning and Multi-Agent Delegation
summary: This paper proposes EvoAgent - an evolvable large language model (LLM) agent framework that integrates structured skill learning with a hierarchical sub-agent delegation mechanism. EvoAgent models skills as multi-file structured capability units equipped with triggering mechanisms and evolutionary metadata, and enables continuous skill generation and optimization through a user-feedback-driven closed-loop process. In addition, by incorporating a three-stage skill matching strategy and a three-layer memory architecture, the framework supports dynamic task decomposition for complex problems and long-term capability accumulation. Experimental results based on real-world foreign trade scenarios demonstrate that, after integrating EvoAgent, GPT5.2 achieves significant improvements in professionalism, accuracy, and practical utility. Under a five-dimensional LLM-as-Judge evaluation protocol, the overall average score increases by approximately 28%. Further model transfer experiments indicate that the performance of an agent system depends not only on the intrinsic capabilities of the underlying model, but also on the degree of synergy between the model and the agent architecture.

AI.tldr: EvoAgent is an evolvable LLM agent framework that structures skills as modular units, supports hierarchical sub-agent delegation, and continually improves via user feedback, yielding large performance gains in real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often lack structured, persistent skills and struggle with complex, decomposed tasks and long-term accumulation of capabilities; the authors want a framework where skills are explicit, modular, and can evolve over time, improving real-world task performance beyond relying only on the base model.
AI.method: They design EvoAgent, which (1) represents skills as multi-file structured capability units with triggers and evolutionary metadata; (2) uses a user-feedback-driven closed-loop to continually generate and optimize skills; (3) introduces a three-stage skill matching strategy plus a three-layer memory architecture to enable dynamic task decomposition and long-term skill accumulation; and (4) integrate the framework with existing LLMs like GPT5.2 and test in real-world foreign trade scenarios using an LLM-as-Judge evaluation protocol.
AI.result: When GPT5.2 is integrated into EvoAgent and applied to real-world foreign trade tasks, it shows notable improvements in professionalism, accuracy, and practical utility, with an approximately 28% increase in overall average score across five evaluation dimensions. Transfer experiments further show that agent system performance is influenced both by the base model’s intrinsic ability and by how well the model and agent architecture are aligned.
AI.conclusion: EvoAgent demonstrates that making skills explicit, modular, and evolvable—combined with hierarchical delegation, structured memory, and feedback-driven optimization—can substantially enhance LLM agents’ real-world effectiveness, and that the synergy between an LLM and its agent framework is as important as the raw strength of the underlying model.

title: Bootstrapping Post-training Signals for Open-ended Tasks via Rubric-based Self-play on Pre-training Text
summary: Self-play has recently emerged as a promising paradigm to train Large Language Models (LLMs). In self-play, the target LLM creates the task input (e.g., ask a question), which it then addresses itself by producing a task output (e.g., give an answer). A reward model evaluates the output, and the rewards are then used to train the LLM, typically via Reinforcement Learning (RL). Self-play incurs minimal supervision costs, and this is especially helpful for post-training LLMs, which require high-quality input-output pairs that traditionally have to be written by humans or expensive proprietary models. However, existing work explores self-play only for verifiable tasks such as math and coding. Instead, we seek to extend it to more realistic open-ended tasks. In particular, we propose POP, a self-play framework that uses the same LLM to synthesize evaluation rubrics, along with input-output pairs, for each example. The rubric is then used to evaluate outputs and train the model. We further ground the framework on a content-rich pretraining corpus to (1) ensure a generation-verification gap and reduce reward hacking, and (2) prevent mode collapse. On Qwen-2.5-7B, POP increases performance of both pretrained and instruction-tuned models, across different tasks ranging from long-form Healthcare QA to creative writing and instruction following.

AI.tldr: POP is a self-play framework where a single LLM generates tasks, answers, and grading rubrics, then learns from rubric-based rewards, extending self-play beyond verifiable domains (math/code) to open-ended tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-play for LLMs focuses on tasks with easily verifiable answers (math, coding) because automatic reward assignment is straightforward there. However, many practically important LLM applications are open-ended (e.g., long-form QA, creative writing) where correctness is subjective and hard to verify. Collecting high-quality human or proprietary-model feedback for such tasks is costly, especially in post-training. The authors want a scalable, low-supervision way to train LLMs on realistic open-ended tasks using self-play, while avoiding pitfalls like reward hacking and mode collapse.
AI.method: They propose POP, a self-play training framework in which a single LLM plays three roles per example: (1) it generates an open-ended task input and a candidate output; (2) it synthesizes an explicit evaluation rubric describing criteria to judge outputs; and (3) it uses that rubric to score outputs via a reward model, providing training signals for reinforcement learning. POP is grounded in a large, content-rich pretraining corpus: generations are conditioned or constrained using that corpus to maintain a gap between generation and verification, which reduces reward hacking, and to ensure diversity to avoid mode collapse. The method is instantiated on the Qwen-2.5-7B model for both base (pretrained) and instruction-tuned variants, and applied across multiple open-ended tasks.
AI.result: When applied to Qwen-2.5-7B, POP improves performance for both the pretrained and instruction-tuned versions of the model. Gains are observed on a variety of open-ended benchmarks, including long-form Healthcare QA, creative writing tasks, and general instruction-following evaluations. The improvements indicate that rubric-based self-play can reliably provide useful reward signals even without external human or proprietary model supervision.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that self-play can be successfully extended from narrowly verifiable tasks to broad open-ended language tasks by having the LLM generate explicit evaluation rubrics and grounding the process in a rich corpus. POP delivers consistent performance improvements with minimal supervision cost, mitigates reward hacking and mode collapse, and offers a scalable path for post-training LLMs on complex, realistic use cases.

title: HiPO: Hierarchical Preference Optimization for Adaptive Reasoning in LLMs
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Includes ablation study across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct on 5 math reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, Minerva, AIME24, Gaokao2023). GPT-4.1 used for structured evaluation of reasoning quality
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is an effective framework for aligning large language models with human preferences, but it struggles with complex reasoning tasks. DPO optimizes for the likelihood of generating preferred over dispreferred responses in their entirety and lacks the granularity to provide feedback on subsections of many-step solutions typical of reasoning tasks. Existing methods excel at either stable preference learning (e.g., DPO variants like KTO and RSO) or structured reasoning (e.g., ReMA's multi-agent RL framework, Tree of Thoughts), but fail to merge these complementary strengths. We propose HiPO (Hierarchical Preference Optimization), an extension of DPO that separates responses into reasoning segments (query clarification and context, reasoning steps, and answer) and computes loss as a weighted sum of the DPO loss for each segment. Our approach enables segment-specific training while maintaining DPO's computational efficiency and training stability. We demonstrate that for multiple 7B LLMs fine-tuned using HiPO and DPO on the Math Stack Exchange preference dataset, the models trained with HiPO outperform the others on a variety of common math benchmarks and achieve greater organization, logical flow, and consistency as measured by GPT-4.1.

AI.tldr: HiPO extends Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) by training on separate reasoning segments (clarification/context, step-by-step reasoning, final answer) with a weighted segment-wise preference loss, improving complex math reasoning and solution organization over vanilla DPO on 7B models.
AI.motivation: Standard DPO aligns language models with preferences at the full-response level, which is coarse-grained and ill-suited for multi-step reasoning where parts of a solution can be good while others are faulty. Existing methods either focus on stable preference learning or explicit reasoning structures, but not both. The authors aim to create a method that preserves DPO’s stability and efficiency while providing fine-grained, structure-aware feedback that better supports complex reasoning tasks such as math problem solving.
AI.method: They introduce HiPO (Hierarchical Preference Optimization), which first decomposes each model response into three functional segments: (1) query clarification and context, (2) reasoning steps, and (3) final answer. For preference pairs, instead of computing a single DPO loss on the full sequence, HiPO computes a DPO-style loss for each segment separately and then aggregates them as a weighted sum, allowing different importance weights for different segments. Training remains offline and DPO-like, but gradients now reflect segment-level preferences, enabling the model to learn better local structure within solutions while retaining DPO’s stability and compute efficiency.
AI.result: Using the Math Stack Exchange preference dataset, they fine-tune several 7B-parameter LLMs with both standard DPO and HiPO. HiPO-trained models outperform vanilla DPO models on multiple standard math benchmarks. Additionally, evaluations by GPT-4.1 indicate that HiPO produces solutions with better organization, more coherent logical flow, and higher consistency than those trained with standard DPO.
AI.conclusion: Segmenting responses into hierarchical reasoning components and applying a weighted, segment-wise DPO loss leads to better alignment for complex reasoning tasks than full-response DPO. HiPO preserves the computational and stability benefits of DPO while adding structure-awareness, yielding performance and qualitative improvements on math reasoning benchmarks and suggesting that preference optimization should be performed at a finer granularity than whole responses for multi-step tasks.

title: SkillLearnBench: Benchmarking Continual Learning Methods for Agent Skill Generation on Real-World Tasks
summary: Skills have become the de facto way to enable LLM agents to perform complex real-world tasks with customized instructions, workflows, and tools, but how to learn them automatically and effectively remains unclear. We introduce SkillLearnBench, the first benchmark for evaluating continual skill learning methods, comprising 20 verified, skill-dependent tasks across 15 sub-domains derived from a real-world skill taxonomy , evaluated at three levels: skill quality, execution trajectory, and task outcome. Using this benchmark, we evaluate recent continual learning techniques, those leveraging one-shot, self/teacher feedback, and skill creator to generate skills from agent experiences. We find that all continual learning methods improve over the no-skill baseline, yet consistent gains remain elusive: no method leads across all tasks and LLMs, and scaling to stronger LLMs does not reliably help. Continual learning improves tasks with clear, reusable workflows but struggles on open-ended tasks, and using stronger LLM backbones does not consistently produce better skills. Our analysis also revealed that multiple iterations in continual learning facilitate genuine improvement via external feedback, whereas self-feedback alone induces recursive drift. Our data and code are open-source at https://github.com/cxcscmu/SkillLearnBench to enable further studies of automatic skill generation and continual learning techniques.

AI.tldr: Benchmark and analyze methods for continual automatic skill learning for LLM agents via SkillLearnBench.
AI.motivation: LLM agents rely on skills to execute complex real-world tasks, but current ways of automatically learning such skills are ad hoc and not well-understood. There is no standard benchmark to compare methods, nor clarity on when continual skill learning works or fails.
AI.method: Create SkillLearnBench, a benchmark of 20 verified, skill-dependent tasks across 15 sub-domains aligned with a real-world skill taxonomy. Define three evaluation levels: skill quality, execution trajectory, and task outcome. Use this benchmark to empirically compare several families of continual skill learning approaches, including recent continual learning algorithms, one-shot and feedback-based approaches (self- and teacher-feedback), and automatic skill creators that derive skills from agent trajectories, across different LLM backbones.
AI.result: All continual learning methods provide improvements over an LLM without learned skills, but no single method dominates across all tasks or models. Benefits are strongest on tasks with clear, reusable workflows and weaker on open-ended tasks. Scaling to more capable LLMs does not consistently yield better learned skills. Multi-iteration continual learning with external feedback tends to yield genuine skill improvement, while self-feedback alone often leads to recursive errors and degradation (drift).
AI.conclusion: SkillLearnBench exposes the challenges of robust, general continual skill learning for LLM agents: while skills help and continual learning adds value, current methods are brittle, task-dependent, and not reliably improved by stronger base models. Effective automatic skill generation appears to require structured tasks and external feedback. The open-sourced benchmark, data, and code provide a foundation for future methods and more systematic study of continual skill learning for LLM agents.

title: Stateless Decision Memory for Enterprise AI Agents
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Companion paper to "Four-Axis Decision Alignment for Long-Horizon Enterprise AI Agents" (arXiv:TBD). Code and reproducibility artifacts at https://github.com/vasundras/stateless-decision-memory-enterprise-ai-agents
summary: Enterprise deployment of long-horizon decision agents in regulated domains (underwriting, claims adjudication, tax examination) is dominated by retrieval-augmented pipelines despite a decade of increasingly sophisticated stateful memory architectures. We argue this reflects a hidden requirement: regulated deployment is load-bearing on four systems properties (deterministic replay, auditable rationale, multi-tenant isolation, statelessness for horizontal scale), and stateful architectures violate them by construction. We propose Deterministic Projection Memory (DPM): an append-only event log plus one task-conditioned projection at decision time. On ten regulated decisioning cases at three memory budgets, DPM matches summarization-based memory at generous budgets and substantially outperforms it when the budget binds: at a 20x compression ratio, DPM improves factual precision by +0.52 (Cohen's h=1.17, p=0.0014) and reasoning coherence by +0.53 (h=1.13, p=0.0034), paired permutation, n=10. DPM is additionally 7-15x faster at binding budgets, making one LLM call at decision time instead of N. A determinism study of 10 replays per case at temperature zero shows both architectures inherit residual API-level nondeterminism, but the asymmetry is structural: DPM exposes one nondeterministic call; summarization exposes N compounding calls. The audit surface follows the same one-versus-N pattern: DPM logs two LLM calls per decision while summarization logs 83-97 on LongHorizon-Bench. We conclude with TAMS, a practitioner heuristic for architecture selection, and a failure analysis of stateful memory under enterprise operating conditions. The contribution is the argument that statelessness is the load-bearing property explaining enterprise's preference for weaker but replayable retrieval pipelines, and that DPM demonstrates this property is attainable without the decisioning penalty retrieval pays.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Deterministic Projection Memory (DPM), a stateless memory architecture that satisfies key regulatory requirements and outperforms traditional summarization-based stateful memory for long-horizon decisions in enterprise settings.
AI.motivation: Enterprise use of long-horizon decision agents in regulated domains still relies on retrieval-augmented, largely stateless pipelines, even though more advanced stateful memory architectures exist. The authors hypothesize that this is not due to conservatism or lagging adoption, but to hidden regulatory and operational requirements: deterministic replay of decisions, auditable rationales, strong tenant isolation, and statelessness for horizontal scaling. Existing stateful memory systems inherently break these properties, making them unsuitable for regulated, high-stakes settings. The paper is motivated by the need for a memory architecture that preserves these enterprise-critical properties without sacrificing decision quality.
AI.method: The authors formally characterize four "load-bearing" systems properties required for regulated deployment: deterministic replay, auditable rationale, multi-tenant isolation, and statelessness. They then introduce Deterministic Projection Memory (DPM), which uses (1) an append-only event log to record all relevant interactions and (2) a single task-conditioned projection step at decision time that compresses the log into the context given to the LLM. This replaces multi-step, stateful summarization pipelines. They empirically evaluate DPM against summarization-based memory on ten regulated decision-making cases under three memory budgets. Metrics include factual precision and reasoning coherence, measured under varying compression ratios, as well as latency, determinism (via replay experiments at temperature 0), and auditability (number of LLM calls logged per decision). They also develop TAMS, a heuristic to guide practitioners in choosing between memory architectures, and perform failure analysis of stateful memory under realistic enterprise constraints.
AI.result: Across ten decision cases and three memory budgets, DPM matches the performance of summarization-based memory when context budgets are generous, and significantly outperforms it when the context budget is tight. At a 20x compression ratio, DPM improves factual precision by +0.52 and reasoning coherence by +0.53 (both large effects, Cohen's h≈1.1, with statistically significant p-values). It is also 7–15x faster under binding budgets, because it requires only a single LLM call at decision time rather than N calls for iterative summarization. Determinism experiments with 10 replays per case (temperature 0) show that both architectures suffer from residual nondeterminism at the API level, but DPM exposes only one nondeterministic step instead of many compounding steps. Similarly, the audit surface is much smaller: DPM logs 2 LLM calls per decision, while summarization logs 83–97 calls on LongHorizon-Bench.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues that statelessness—rather than sheer retrieval strength—is the key property driving enterprises to favor retrieval-augmented pipelines in regulated domains. Stateful memory architectures intrinsically undermine deterministic replay, auditability, isolation, and scalable deployment. Deterministic Projection Memory demonstrates that one can retain effective long-horizon decision quality while satisfying these properties: it attains or exceeds the performance of summarization-based memory, with better determinism, auditability, and latency. The authors conclude that DPM offers a practical path to deployable, regulation-compliant long-horizon agents and encapsulate their insights in TAMS, a heuristic for selecting appropriate memory architectures and understanding the failure modes of stateful memory in enterprise environments.

title: Less Languages, Less Tokens: An Efficient Unified Logic Cross-lingual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Framework
summary: Cross-lingual chain-of-thought (XCoT) with self-consistency markedly enhances multilingual reasoning, yet existing methods remain costly due to extensive sampling of full trajectories across languages. Moreover, multilingual LLM representations vary strongly by language, hindering direct feature comparisons and effective pruning. Motivated by this, we introduce UL-XCoT, the first efficient unified logic cross-lingual reasoning framework that minimizes redundancy in token usage and latency, yielding the greatest efficiency under limited sampling budgets during inference. Specifically, UL-XCoT (1) achieves less languages by selecting, per query, a small candidate language set in a language-invariant unified logic space, (2) enables less tokens by monitoring logic-space trajectory dynamics during decoding to prune low-quality reasoning paths, and (3) aggregates the remaining high-quality trajectories via voting. Experiments on PolyMath across 18 languages and MMLU-ProX-Lite across 29 languages with DeepSeek-R1-DistillQwen-7B demonstrate that UL-XCoT achieves competitive accuracy while sharply cutting over 50% decoding token cost versus prior sampling baselines. UL-XCoT also delivers more stable gains on low-resource languages, underscoring consistently superior robustness where standard XCoT self-consistency method fails.

AI.tldr: UL-XCoT is an efficient cross-lingual chain-of-thought framework that reduces token and language redundancy while maintaining strong multilingual reasoning performance.
